coredata.dc:title: Multidimensional nanoadditives in tribology
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Na
Chen, Yang
Lin, Haibo
liaquat, Hassan
Zhang, Feizhi
Yang, Kang
coredata.dc:description: Achieving low friction and high wear-resistant has been regarded as the most concerned topics in the scientific progress of nano-tribo materials, that attained significant attentions for reducing energy expenditure and optimizing wear-critical applications. Despite numerous research activities on nano-additives for tribological optimizations, subdividing them from 0D to 3D nanostructures for the transformation of experiment achievements to industrial-scale applications remains rarely explored. Therefore, a comprehensive review of up-to-date survey in nanoadditives for realizing low friction and wear, or even superlubric state in the solid/liquid lubrication systems from the nanoscale to the macroscale is presented. Finally, this review is concluded by providing future outlooks on key challenges and valuable recommendations for a myriad of existing and future engineering-scale applications.

Wear-resistant applications
Anti-wear mechanisms
Super-lubricity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Achieving low friction and high wear-resistant has been regarded as the most concerned topics in the scientific progress of nano-tribo materials, that attained significant attentions for reducing energy expenditure and optimizing wear-critical applications. Despite numerous research activities on nano-additives for tribological optimizations, subdividing them from 0D to 3D nanostructures for the transformation of experiment achievements to industrial-scale applications remains rarely explored. Therefore, a comprehensive review of up-to-date survey in nanoadditives for realizing low friction and wear, or even superlubric state in the solid/liquid lubrication systems from the nanoscale to the macroscale is presented. Finally, this review is concluded by providing future outlooks on key challenges and valuable recommendations for a myriad of existing and future engineering-scale applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Large-scale use of energy is intertwined with societal successful development, which has improved people's living standards in many aspects [1]. Over the years, on the one hand, energy has powered our everything from fundamental necessities to technology innovations; on the other hand, increasing activities of the industrial sectors have also supported by such energy sources. However, despite energy has provided great progress in human life and industrial events, we still do not use it efficiently [2–4]. For instance, most energy is successively and excessively expended, that releases toxic substances and increased carbon/nitrogen-oxide emissions, resulting in energy scarcity and environment problems [5–7]. Meanwhile, due to the energy waste from wear, the fiscal waste amounts to approximately 6% of GDP in present industrialized countries [8]. Moreover, improper allocation of the energy also disproportionately affects the ecosystems [9,10]. Overcoming these barriers to efficiently utilize energy is critical for a rapid development of community. As an example, even a modest 20% reduction in friction consumption can significantly reduce real economic costs in terms of energy use and environmental benefits [11,12]. Therefore, along this perspective, exploring effective strategies to control friction and wear is of scientific and engineering value for a green and sustainable future [13–15].

In this regard, nano-additives have been widely explored for much of industrial-scaled tribology applications due to their varied morphologies, excellent properties in electric, mechanics, thermodynamic aspects [16–19], etc. According to the spatial structures, nanoadditives can be generally classified into four categories, namely, zero-dimensional (0D), one-dimensional (1D), two-dimensional (2D), and three-dimensional (3D) architectures [20,21]. Specifically, 0D-nanoadditives such as approximate nanodots, nanoparticles, and nanospheres exhibit all dimensions in the nanometer scale range (about 1–500 nm) [22]; 1D-nanoadditives, including nanowires, nanofibers, nanorods, nanotubes, and nanobelts have two nano-scaled dimensions with high length-to-diameter aspect ratios [23,24]; 2D-nanoadditive members involve ultrathin sheet-like materials, like various flakes, films, multilayers and their correspondingznetworks, whose thickness is controlled in the nanoscale range, from a few to dozens of nanometers [25,26]; 3D-nanoadditives, having all dimensions beyond the nanometer range, are commonly defined as bulk materials consisted of single or more basic structural units of 0D, 1D, and 2D-nanomaterials [27,28] (Fig. 1 ).

As one of nanomaterials, 0D-nanoadditives have been demonstrated to be well suitable for a wide range of tribological applications in both solid and liquid forms, owing to their unique spherical nanostructures, high electron mobility and availability of the chemical modifications [37–39]. These beneficial factors effectively promote the 0D-nanomaterial applications in many important fields, like electricity, medicine, and superlubricity, etc.

Similar to the 0D-nanoadditives, numerous studies regarding the large-scale utilization of 1D and 2D-nanoadditives have attracted great attentions in the field of tribology [40,41]. Improvements in tribological behaviors of the nanoadditive-reinforced solid/liquid matrices mainly relies on their inherent characteristics [42], for instance their dimensions, dispersion states, and the bonding strength with matrices. Based on these characteristics, the frictional performances of materials have recently attained an innovative development, namely, superlubricity behavior. Superlubricity, defined as a state of friction coefficient between two contacting surfaces below 0.01 or even close to zero, has been considered as a viable strategy to relieve the adverse effects of friction and wear (such as mechanical failures, energy losses, and environmental pollution) [43,44]. As a special phenomenon in solid/liquid lubrication, superlubricating behaviors of nanomaterials also deserve more attention beyond conventional lubrication performance. 1D-nanomaterials with high aspect ratios and 2D-nanomaterials having weak van der Waals forces are considered as ideal materials for achieving superlubricity [45]. In the case of incommensurate contact, 1D-nanomaterials can serve as the “smoothest bearings” to nearly vanish friction and wear for suppression of stick-slip motion [46]. 2D-nanomaterials can provide low shear strength and make adjacent layers slide easily against each other during wear, thus resulting in an excellent lubrication or even superlubricity [47].

Besides, 3D-nanomaterials inherit good characteristics of their components to show excellent lubrication [48,49]. Unlike 0D, 1D and 2D-additives, 3D-nanoadditives exhibit attractive surface areas and tunable structures [50]. Designing the 3D-structured nanomaterials can effectively improve their tribological behaviors, and even obtain the expected superlubricity, which enable their unique applications in unexploited areas such as biomedicine, cosmetics, etc.

As rapid progress of nanotechnology, the unique structures and good physicochemical performances of nanomaterials make them become the research hotspots, thus, they can serve as promising additives in solid/liquid matrices for achieving good lubricity [40,51]. Compared with conventional additives (like zinc dialkyl dithiophosphate (ZDDP), ammonium salts of dialkyldithiophosphoric acid, etc.), the nanoadditives perform the advantages of small sizes and high specific surface areas, that are not only conducive to improving the interface compatibility between the additives and solid matrix, but also easy to enter the friction interface in liquid lubrication system, thus providing excellent lubricating effect [52]. Moreover, the mechanical, chemical and geometric properties of nanoadditives play the crucial roles in almost all of these growing applications [53–55]. Despite these properties have been proved to be critical to the impressive properties of nanoadditives, the tribological potentials of nanoadditives and their relationships to industrial-scale applications, remain to be fully explored. Therefore, in this review, the superior tribological properties of nanoadditives in solid/liquid lubrication systems are summarized; meanwhile, their important contributions for superlubricity are discussed. It begins with a brief introduction of advanced structures and properties of nanomaterials. In Section 2, significant applications of wear-resistant ability and friction reduction for 0D-nanoadditives are elaborated. Afterward, improved tribological behaviors of material system by using 1D- and 2D-nanoadditives are discussed in Sections 3 and 4, respectively. Section 5 concludes the increasing efforts regarding excellent lubrication behaviors of 3D-nanoadditives. Finally, the most valuable conclusions and outlooks of nanoadditives in tribology are provided in Section 6.

For exploring the effective tribological optimization strategies, 0D-nanoadditivess, for instance Al2O3, CeO2, ZnO and carbon-based quantum dots, have been extensively applied into various solid/liquid matrices (such as metals, polymers, ceramics, and lubricants) for controlling friction and wear [56–59]. Moreover, these nanoadditives, when mixed with fuel, play an important role in improving engine performance and reducing engine emissions (i.e., hydrocarbons, carbon oxides, and nitrogen oxides); portion of them also exhibits potential application prospects in the cosmetic, sunscreen, and food additive industries [59,60]. Unique near-spherical structure and nanoscale dimension of the 0D-nanoadditives endow material systems with excellent tribological potential, that is good lubricity [61–64] or even superlubricity [65]. Table 1 summarizes detailed information on the tribological reports of 0D-nanoadditives reinforced composite systems. In the following sections, the wear-resistant actions and friction reduction for 0D-nanoadditives in solid and liquid systems are described, and the superlubricity mechanisms of 0D-enhanced systems are discussed emphatically.

Much of nanomaterials that serve as functional additives for optimizing the properties of solid matrices have attracted great interest. Attentions to nanoadditives accelerated incentives for the applications of nanodots, nanoparticles, and nanospheres in solid materials for achieving the large-scaled optimizations, including mechanical behavior, anti-wear performance, and friction reduction ability [66–68]. It is noteworthy that, these strengthening effects are closely related to the interactions in tribo-systems.

Based on recent reports, the interactions for tribo-systems can be grouped mainly into two categories: internal and interface interactions [69]. Internal interactions refer to the attraction, repulsion, and reaction between the nanoadditive and the matrix inside the composite. In these effects, the surface characteristics of nanoadditives and the chemical composition of matrix perform the dominate roles. Interface effects involve the sliding, rolling and reaction of additives on the matrix surface. These processes can be mainly controlled by the morphological, mechanical and chemical properties of the interacting bodies. Therefore, the matrix chemistry and additive morphology should also be focused on, other than the mechanical properties of the interacting materials, to determine friction and wear mechanisms.

Metal-based composites: Compared to microparticles, nanosized 0D-particles exhibit better bonding strength with the matrix due to size effect, and the pinning effect can refine the matrix grain, and inhibit the dislocation movement, thus effectively improving the mechanical and tribological properties of the nano-composite [70,71]. For the case of solid composite systems, the amounts of nanoparticles in composites are more than that of microparticles under similar mass fractions, that results in a decrease in the distance between nanoparticles and an increase in the interaction area between the nanoparticles and their matrixes [72], thereby providing a viable strategy for improving the interface bonding strength of composite system. For metal substrates, 0D-nanoadditives serving as reinforcements can be pinned to the crystal boundary due to their small sizes, effectively fixing the grain boundary of matrix, inhibiting the growth of crystal grains, and restricting the motions of grain boundaries [73]. Improved bonding strength, refined grains, and dislocation motion restriction attached great importance to the enhancements in mechanical and tribological properties of the solid composites [74].

Dispersion state of 0D-material and the interfacial bonding strength with their matrixes are directly responsible for the tribological behaviors of composite systems [75]. Owing to well-dispersed nano-Al2O3 in Al-Mg alloys, the tribological behaviors of composites were significantly improved compared with that of pure alloys [76]. An important factor for the observable reduction in wear rate (2.7 times) was the improved hardness caused by grain refinement. However, Ceschini et al. [77] showed that despite utilizing Al2O3 nanoparticles, a significant grain refinement was induced, but the fatigue life of their composites would be lower with respect to the unreinforced AA7005 alloy. In this regard, TiO2 nanoparticles-containing AA5083 alloys achieved high hardness and ultimate tensile strength, as well as attractive tribological properties [78]. The reduced grain-sizes, uniform dispersion of nano-TiO2 particles, and dislocation strengthening substantially improved the wear-resisting capacity. Notably, the structural integrity of nanoadditives in lubricating systems also performed a dominant role in their lubricating behavior.

Multifarious convention techniques, such as liquid metallurgy, thermal spray and powder metallurgy, have been proposed to fabricate metal matrix composites [79,80]. However, the inevitable high temperature during the preparation process might affect the structural integrity of nanoadditives in metal base system, resulting in their enhancement effect not as expected. Motivated by this, high-temperature-resistant reinforcements and reasonable preparation techniques have increasingly aroused the interest of researchers [81,82]. High strength, strong hardness and excellent thermal stability of nano-ceramics make them promising candidates for reinforcement. Amra et al. [83] incorporated nano-sized CeO2 with SiC-particles to enhance the wear-resistance action of Al5083-alloy, using friction stir processing (FSP). Typical patterns of FSP-equipment for synthesising Al5083 were clearly shown in Fig. 2a . Fig. 2b–e systematically demonstrated that as the FSP duration increased, the grain sizes of nanocomposites were gradually refined. All the composites exhibiting higher hardness and wear-resistance compared to the metal matrix have been found into Fig. 2f, and the best friction-reducing and antiwear actions of Al5083/CeO2/SiC were also proved, among these as-prepared composites. Synergistic enhancements of the FSP and nanosized-ZrO2 added in AZ31-alloys were analyzed by Mazaheri et al. [84]. Significant microhardness improvements of the composites via adding ZrO2 nanoparticles were discovered in Fig. 2 g. By performing the four passes FSP with adding ZrO2, the acquired hardness of composite was potentially improved by nearly 90%. Increasing hardness contributed to the optimization of wear resistance. Obviously, mass-loss of based-metal (BM) was higher than in other samples, as shown in Fig. 2h. The uniform distribution of ZrO2 and increasing passes of FSP reduced the AZ31 mass-loss and induced excellent wear-resistant and antifriction features, corresponding 40%-reduction in wear rates and 50%-decrease in friction coefficients.

Interface as a "bridge" between the additives and substrates, performs a vital role in the physical and chemical properties of composites. For metal-based nanocomposites, low interfacial interactions of the nano-ceramic particles with respect to their matrixes remain open to researchers. This is because the bonding types between the particles and matrixes are not available at a molecular level. Surface modifications of additives provide a feasible strategy for improving the interfacial bonding of composites [4]. Conventional methods, including physical techniques (e.g. coating [85] and dipping [86]) and chemical routes (e.g. electroplating [87], heat treatment [88], and chemical treatment [89]), have been developed to endow material surfaces with a wide range of targeted properties. Because of their high specific surface areas and energies, 0D-nanoadditives usually have a strong tendency for self-aggregation. These methods can not only deeply develop the intrinsic properties of nanoadditives, but also provide excellent dispersion and adhesion for reinforcements. The strategy of Ag coating nano-Al2O3 was proposed to improve the wettability between Cu and Al2O3 [82]. After testing at a velocity of 1 m/s and an applied load of 10 N, the Cu-8 wt% Al2O3 composite demonstrated a 60% improvement in wear performance with respect to Cu base. This was attributable to the good interface bonding strength between Ag-Al2O3 and Cu matrix, and the uniform dispersion of Ag-Al2O3 in matrix, that inhibited the plastic deformation and removal ability of Cu, thereby reducing abrasive wear rate. However, surface modifications greatly increase the manufacturing cost of nanocomposites; meanwhile, the interface bonding strength of the composite is strongly dependent on Ag coverage; thus, it puts forward new requirements for the synthetic process, aiming to reduce costs and simplify processes.

As an important interface effect, wear-induced elastic-plastic deformation of composite is responsible for the gradual extrusion of nanoadditives from matrix [90,91]. The sustained deformation and mechanical action result in the accumulation of nanoadditives at tribo-interface, promoting the formation of nanoadditive-incorporated tribofilm. A host of works have reported the contributions of 0D-nanoadditives on tribofilm formation, and believed that the high-performance tribofilm formed above wear interface to play a crucial role in enhancing tribological behaviors [92,93]. According to the descriptions of Bian et al. [90], macroscopical friction coefficients of tribofilms were appropriately predicted using the following formulas:

From the as-mentioned formulas above, nanoadditives promoted the formation of a tribo-film with the optimized coefficient of friction, which were crucial to excellent tribological behaviors of the composites. Load-bearing tribofilm with the easy-shearing characteristic hindered firsthand contacts of the mating pairs, hence, friction-reduction and wear-resistant abilities of composites were enhanced. Nanosized graphite particles (35 nm) reinforced Cu composites provided better wear resistance and lower friction coefficients than those of micron-scale ones [94], which were primarily related with the nanographite particles embedded into the Cu-matrix to form high adherent graphite-layer on the contact surfaces. Such protective film contributed to the reduction in subsurface deformation of the composites. Further, the crucial contribution of nano-Bi2O3 particles to tribofilm has been reported by Guo et al. [95]. Under a pressure of 1 MPa and the sliding speeds of 0.05–0.2 m/s, nano-Bi2O3 on the friction interface provided an effective strategy to improve load-bearing ability of the tribofilm, hence, significant enhancements in wear-resistant actions were achieved in composites.

Polymer-based composites: Akin to metal-based composites, surface characteristics of the 0D-nanoadditives are also of great importance for their interfacial interaction with the polymer matrix (especially the nucleation ability). That is, the nanoadditives restrict the mobility of the polymer chains in their vicinity and thereby influence the overall matrix performance [69]. Padhan et al. [96] reported the tribological performance of PAEK (poly aryl ether ketone) reinforced with functionalized nano-Al2O3. Such nanoparticles resulted in the significantly improved mechanical behaviors (strength and dynamic mechanical properties) in every case because of the enhanced adhesion with the polymer matrix, that minimized the chances of being thrown out in wear debris. Hence, the ultra-low wear rate of 2 × 10−16 m3/ Nm, and friction coefficient for 0.044 were observed.

Ceramic-based composites: 0D-nanoparticles have made outstanding contributions to tribological optimization of the ceramic matrixes [97–100]. The size-scaled influence of nanoadditive contributed to repairing micro-defects of matrixes, refining the grain size of the matrix, and optimizing mechanical and tribological behaviors of composite systems. In this case, nanoadditive contents perform a leading role in strengthening mechanism. Mechanical and tribological properties of nanocomposites such as CrN/Ag, ZrN/Ag, and TiN/Ag with different Ag contents have been determined via performing nanoindentation and wear tests, respectively [97,98]. Friction coefficients decreased as the Ag contents increased, whereas the wear resistance of coating decreased gradually. This was mainly attributed to the self-agglomeration of high content of Ag in the matrix, resulted in the weakening of self-repairing effect and the surface hardness deterioration for composite. Similarly, for nano-Ni particles enhanced ceramic composite coatings [100], an optimal content of about 10 wt%-Ni caused the friction and wear of composite to be decreased by 16.6% and 45.4%, respectively, as compared to the pure ceramic sample. However, as exceeding 10 wt%, the agglomerated Ni-nanoparticles might be observed to pull-out from the coating surface during the friction process, thus led to the poor surface quality and a deteriorated wear resistant.

Meanwhile, with respect to the ceramic-based composites, reasonable contents of nanoadditives also perform the crucial roles in the formation of tribofilm. For the CrN-Ag composite coatings, various nano-Ag atomic ratios (0, 3, 12 and 22 at.%) rendered the composite to show different tribological behaviors [99]. After 100 cycles of reciprocating at 0.7 mm/s and applying a normal force of 1 N, the 22 at.%-Ag enabled the coating to achieve consistently low friction coefficients (0.04–0.06), owing to low shear strength of Ag and the easily formed tribofilm across the frictional interface.

According to above-mentioned discussions, the tribological optimization mechanism of additives for 0D-composites depends greatly on the chemical properties of the matrix. Small-sized additive is beneficial to pin into the metal grain boundary, which provides a feasible strategy for refining grain and improving matrix hardness and wear resistance. For the ceramic, suitable content of 0D-additive contributed to repairing the microdefects of the matrix, inhibiting the nucleation of cracks, and improving the friction and wear behavior of the composite system. Due to the chain structure in polymer, the introduction of 0D-material provides a nucleation site for composite system, where the polymer chain entangles and nucleates around the additive, thus endowing the composite improved surface hardness and anti-wear performance.

The 0D-nanoadditives are not only suitable for large-scaled tribological applications in solid form, but have also been demonstrated to perform well in liquid lubrication [101–103]. The excellent lubrication ability of lubricants is directly responsible for the working efficiency, application accuracy and service life of many moving contact parts. Consequently, important discussions on the 0D-nanoadditives for promoting the antiwear and friction reduction performances of liquid lubrication systems are crucial particularly [104–106].

Recently, 0D-nanoadditives have been extensively explored as novel nano-lubricant additives. For example, Tang et al. [107] were the first group that reported the use of water-based lubricants in the amorphous carbon (a-C) contacts by mixing 0.1 wt% carbon dots (CDs). The friction coefficients and wear rates of the a-C contacts were reduced by 33% and 80%, respectively, if compared to those of pure water-lubricated systems. In reality, considering the limitation in manufacturing technique, the surfaces of tribo-pairs cannot be completely smooth at the micro-/nano-scales. The unique near-spherical structure and extremely small size endowed 0D-particles with a nano-rolling mechanism and surface repair ability. They were prone to enter the frictional interface, fill the surface pits, repair interface defects, further well improve the tribo-performances [107–109]. In comparison with pure deionized (DI) water, the friction and wear behaviors of CDs water solution obtained the notable improvements [108]. The existence of CDs played the ball-bearing role, and transformed the sliding friction into rolling friction, thus realizing low friction coefficients and wear rates (Fig. 3a ). However, the low interactions of tribo-surfaces and poor thermal stability limited the industrialization applications of 0D nanometer additives in liquid lubrication.

As a result, full understandings regarding the influences of working temperatures on the lubrication behaviors of nanoadditive-reinforced liquid systems are important considerably [81,110]. As such, exploration on the tribological properties of Ag-doped carbon quantum dots (Ag-CQDs) as liquid additive has been conducted over a wide temperature range by Wang et al. [110]. At low-temperature, an incorporation of 0.05 wt% Ag-CQDs was proven to achieve significant decreases of 42.2% in the friction coefficient and 35.3% in wear scar diameter, as opposed to the base liquid. Furthermore, Ag-CQDs substantially reduced friction and wear when the ambient temperature was greater than 200 °C. The important reasons for tribological improvements were further presented. When the temperature being relatively low, the metal worn-surface was easy to excite low energy electrons under shear action, thus forming positive charges. Subsequent Ag-CQDs additives were rapidly deposited and embedded into the wear surface through physical absorption and electrostatic attraction, eventually reducing friction and repairing the rubbing surfaces. Under elevated temperatures, combined actions of the pressure and friction heat could induce the complex tribo-chemical reactions on the tribo-contact areas; as a consequence, the reaction-induced protective layer composed of metal native oxides, carbides, nitrides, silver compound and their derivatives, could be formed on the worn surface successively. Thereinto, the stacked carbon derived from the Ag-CQDs produced large amounts of defects and disordered structures under shear action, formed a graphene-like structure, and endowed the protective layer with superior lubricity at high temperatures. Hard phases, including metal oxides, nitrides, and many amorphous carbides, improved the strength and bearing capacity of the protective layer. Therefore, high-performance protective layer prevented the tribo-surfaces from contacting directly, enabling the contact interface to escape from damages.

In a similar study, Li et al. [111] developed the 2-mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT) based ILs, namely, N, N, N-trimethyl-N-dodecylammonium 2-mercaptobenzothiazolate (N12) and 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium 2-mercaptobenzothiazolate (EM). Then, tribological impacts of Mo nanoparticles and MBT-based ionic liquids (ILs) in the polyethylene glycol (PEG) base oil were investigated for steel/steel contacts at 100 °C and 20 °C. Their results indicated that better anti-friction and wear resistant abilities were obtained by using compound additives under a temperature of 100 °C (Fig. 3b), if compared to those of 20 °C. A possible explanation was that, high temperature (100 °C) and contact pressure activated the tribochemical reactions during friction, where Mo could react with S-based anions from the MBT to form a MoS2-rich tribofilm (Fig. 3c) on the tribo-surfaces, thus achieving excellent synergistic lubrication effects. In summary, it can be concluded from these above studies, both low- and high-temperatures induced the different frictional mechanisms: namely, (i) mainly involving surface repair and nano-rolling effects at low temperatures; (ii) mainly involving high-performance tribochemical protective films at elevated temperatures. These phenomena render the liquid-lubricated systems to exhibit outstanding friction-reducing and wear resistant properties. Further in-depth discussion on the tribochemical protective film induced by a bis(2-ethylhexyl) phosphate-dispersed CeO2 nanolubricant under high temperatures, has been conducted in experimental works of Ali et al. [112].

Note that, bare 0D-nanoadditives usually possess excess surface energy, resulting in them tend to coalesce spontaneously [113]. Appropriate surface modification technique is beneficial to the formation of a relatively stable dispersion of nanoparticles in solution. Depending on the liquid matrix, the basic principle of such strategy is that in the case of an aqueous solvent, the modified particles should be hydrophilic, and be hydrophobic in the case of a low-polar organic medium. It is believed that the stability of entire liquid system stems from the Brownian movement of solvent molecules and the reasonable surface energy for the modified 0D-particles [114–118]. Li et al. [119] investigated tribological properties of nano-LaF3 particle capped by tributyl phosphate (TBP-LaF3) as additives in liquid paraffin. Results testified that the liquid paraffin containing TBP-LaF3 nanoparticle was endowed with excellent antiwear and friction-reducing abilities, and load-carrying property. As a surface modifier, TBP improved the LaF3 dispersion in liquid paraffin, and allowed good adsorption of LaF3 on a sliding steel surface. The adsorbed particles were responsible for the optimized lubricating behavior of the lubricant since they effectively prevented the direct collisions between tribo-pairs.

Recently, hexafluorophosphate (PF6) modifier has been considered to tune the physical and chemical properties of carbon dots-IL (CDs-IL) mixed nanoparticles. The roles of CDs-PF6 (0–2 wt%) on tribological behaviors of composites were systematically studied [109]. Tribological functions of PEG-based fluids were significantly improved with the observed increase of CDs-PF6 content. At a suitable concentration of 0.75 wt%, the anti-wear performance was optimally improved, which corresponding a 97.3% reduction in wear volume of CDs-PF6/PEG, if compared to pure PEG. As the concentration of arriving up to 1 wt%, the wear volume of CDs-PF6/PEG was similar to that at 0.75 wt%, but the friction coefficient was declined by approximately 72.7%, as opposed to pure PEG. As evident from Fig. 3d, the worn surface of a steel ball lubricated using 1 wt% CDs-PF6/PEG was smaller, shallower and smoother than pure PEG. Superior frictional properties were primarily ascribed to unique spherical microstructure of CDs-IL endowed with the “rolling effect”. With the prolonging frictional process, the as-deposited physical adsorption film occurred the decomposition during tribo-chemical process, which not only formed the protective transferring film on the nascent metal surfaces, but also achieved the “self-mending effect” via filling into the deep scratches, further resulting in the excellent anti-wear and friction-reducing properties.

Under hydrodynamic lubrication, the matching rubbing surfaces are separated by the lubricants, main factor in controlling frictional force and load-bearing capacity is the lubricant viscosity. Many researches have reported incremental changes in the viscosities of lubricating oils containing 0D-nanoadditives [120–122]. Evident increase in viscosity resulted in an obvious enhancement in the carrying capacity of compound oil. The report of Afrand et al. [122] revealed significant correlations between lubricant viscosities and nanoparticle concentrations. Increased volume fraction of particles enhanced the viscosity of nanolubricants, and subsequently improved the tribological performances. However, high concentration was unequal to high anti-wear thanks to the lubrication complexity. Further experimental works indicated that, appropriate concentration of 0D-nanomaterial performed the dominate role in determining lubricant viscosity [121].

High concentration nanoparticles also tend to an agglomeration, and thus result in the undesirable decrease in friction and wear properties [107,123]. Therefore, appropriate concentrations of 0D-nanoadditives are the dominant factor for ensuring the outstanding tribological behaviors of lubricants. Zareh-Desari et al. [123] prepared soybean and rapeseed oils with different concentrations of nano-CuO and SiO2. The standard ring compression test was carried out to evaluate the tribological performances of target lubricants (Fig. 4a and b). According to Fig. 4c–F, the most efficient concentrations of nanoparticles were determined within the wide ranges from 0.5 to 0.7 wt%. Adding 0.5 wt% SiO2 into base oils led to the best tribological performances (Fig. 4c and d); in the case of CuO-containing systems, both of 0.5 and 0.7 wt% concentrations have been proved to facilitate the optimal values in friction coefficients (Fig. 4e and F). Based on these improvements in tribological properties, 0D-nanoadditives have been increasingly recognized as ideal lubricant additives.

Comparing with aforementioned 0D-nanoadditives, recently new-style natural polymer cellulose and its derivative materials attracted much of interests, owing to their biocompatibility and biodegradable features. Cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) derived from the natural cellulose, is characterized by high surface area, colloidal stability, low toxicity, and mechanical strength, thus making it a promising 0D-candidate for the production of economy-environment-friendly and high-performance nanolubricants [124–127]. In a relevant study, the roles of CNC concentrations in the tribological behaviors of SAE 40 oil were explored [127]. Compared with those of 0.3, 0.5, 0.7 and 0.9 vol% CNC, the 0.1 wt%-CNC enabled such nano-lubricant to acquire higher kinematic viscosity, that ensured the nanolubricant effectiveness even at high temperature via maintaining the oil film thickness. Thus, 0.1 vol% CNC concentration is more suitable and stable under elevated temperature for better lubrication performances.

In addition, the useful combination of nanocellulose with other nanomaterials is also considered to be feasible way for the improvements of tribological performances of liquid lubricants [128,129]. CNCCuO nanoparticles has been also ensured to significantly improve friction reduction and wear resistant performances of the SAE 40 base oil [130]. More specifically, under low speeds-high loads, despite the low friction coefficients for SAE 40 oil with 0.1, 0.3 and 0.5 vol% CNCCuO, compared with raw oil, their values were relatively large of about 0.08152, 0.08145, 0.08183, respectively. On the contrary, in the conditions of high speeds-low loads, the temperature of friction region was reduced, which made the viscosity increase; thus, the increment of viscosity resulted in smaller friction coefficients, i.e., 0.02363, 0.0206, and 0.02377. In short, for above-mentioned two conditions, 0.3 vol%-CNCCuO was the optimal concentration to reduce the friction and wear of the tribo-pairs. As demonstrated by SEM and EDX studies, the CNCCuO tribo-film was formed on the worn surface, and provided a key aisle in repairing the cracks and the scratches of friction surface. Meanwhile, based on the Cu and O elements on the tribo-interfaces, a chemical reaction between the CNCCuO and metal surface was deduced to appear, that may contribute to a surface polishing effect, thereby achieving the low friction and excellent anti-wear ability.

In conclusion, uniform and stable dispersion is the crucial prerequisite for 0D-additive to achieve the tribological optimization in liquid environment. On this basis, the well dispersed 0D-material provides surface repair, nano-rolling, and surface polishing effects for lubrication system, greatly contributed to reducing friction and wear of tribo-pairs. More importantly, the lubricated worn surfaces proved the tribofilm formation via physical adsorption and complex tribochemical reactions [131], that were helpful in achieving low friction and high anti-wear ability.

Superlubricity, is a fantastic lubrication state in which sliding friction resistance nearly vanishes. Hence, it largely depends on the dynamic friction coefficients between two surfaces during relative motion process. Usually, the superlubricity is referred to dynamic friction coefficients below 0.01 or even close to the vanishing level (0.001 or less) [46,65,132].

Recent reports indicated the superlubricity of 0D-nanoadditives exhibiting an obvious load-dependent. At low contact pressure (<1 GPa), ultrananocrystalline diamond films could show ultralow friction coefficient in humid air [133]; the similar friction behaviors were also observed for hydrogenated amorphous carbon films (<0.45 GPa, in dry N2) [134]. The suspended covalent bond was passivated by adsorption of water molecules in the atmosphere to induce ultra-low friction [135]. Besides that, the tibological behaviors of fullerene-like carbon (FLC) were explored by considering interface transformations under different loads [136]. It observed that the friction coefficients of FLC decreased to below 0.01 only at high load (8 N, 770 Mpa). The frictional surface formed many loosely spherical nanoparticles with outer graphite shells, that reduced the true contact area and realized superlow friction.

However, this interaction may be negligible due to the presence of mechanical effects at high contact pressure (>0.5 Gpa for amorphous carbon films). In this case, the combination of the two (nanocrystalline and amorphous material) interaction provides a feasible strategy for realizing superlubricity [137]. Hydrogenated amorphous carbon film containing nanodiamond particles (ND-a-C:H) achieved the superlubricity under the loads higher than 2 GPa [137]. A feasible mechanism was proposed to explain the tribochemical reactions at the interfaces. The atoms across the ND-a-C:H film interface rearranged to form graphene ribbons or onion-like carbons for such given contact pressure. The in-situ formation of curved graphene ribbons or onion carbon tribofilms at the frictional interface was responsible for the ultra-low friction coefficient. At high contact pressures, mechanical properties (i.e., elastic resilience and hardness) performed the decisive roles for the implementation of atom-by-atom remove at macroscale. Nanodiamond particles embedded in the amorphous carbon network contributed to improving the carrying capacity of the a-C film, inducing the formation of onion carbon, thus resulting in the inappropriate contact of friction interfaces.

Apart from the load-dependent, the superlubricity of nanoparticles also exhibits main dependence on their volume contents [65]. A simple modification strategy has been applied to fabricate ionic liquid/carbon quantum dot (IL-CQD) nanoparticles (Fig. 5a ) [65]. As-fabricated spherical IL-CQDs exhibited excellent dispersibility and dense granular, with a small average diameter of approximately 5.6 nm. IL-CQDs as lubricants demonstrated the excellent tribological performances (Fig. 5b and c), and the details have been presented in Table 1. When the CQDs contents increased to 3.6%, a super-low friction coefficient of approximately 0.006 was observed (Fig. 5b); meanwhile, the wear depth declined down to about 38 nm (Fig. 5c), and the wear rate decreased down to a relatively low value (0.7 × 10−14 mm3/Nm). These values were considerably lower than other chosen IL-CQDs contents (such as 0%, 0.98%, and 8.2%).

Further, the role of temperatures on friction and wear behaviors of nanoadditives has been reported [45]. Fullerene-like hydrogenated carbon (FL-C:H) films with the curved graphene structures were promising materials for achieving superlubricity behavior [138]. However, the ideal superlubricity of FL-C:H films was only determined under room temperature condition. For obtaining their superlubricity at high temperatures, Wang et al. [139] experimentally annealed FL-C:H film in a good N2 protection. The order degree for FL-C:H film increased along with the arguments of annealed temperatures from 200 °C to 500 °C, facilitating graphene in such film to further curl into clusters and aggregation. In particular, heat-treated films under 300 °C were verified to possess the optimal anti-wear and friction-reducing actions, which were associated with the formation of onion-like carbon nanospheres between contact surfaces to reduce the frictional forces.

Overall, it can be ensured from abovementioned reports that the rational manipulation of 0D-nanoadditives for realizing the superlubricity requires the vital considerations of service environments, including applied loads, volume contents, and work temperatures. In addition, under certain conditions, 0D-particles on the contact interface can provide a key pathway for the friction conversion from the sliding to rolling, and form the surface tribofilm, reduce the real contact area, hence inducing the occurrence of super-lubrication phenomenon. Very recently, the successful superlubrication regime has benefited from a tribofilm formation at the friction-contacted interface [132]. Regarding Si3N4 ceramics lubricated with the aqueous lubricant with NH2-SiO2 nanoparticles [132], the friction coefficients of sliding wear tests achieved superlow values following the short running-in process (Fig. 5d). As seen from Fig. 5d combined with SEM images (Fig. 5e and F) of Si3N4 wear surfaces, Fig. 5e corresponded to wear surface after approximately 300 s of friction testing, while Fig. 5f showed the wear surface after achieving superlubricity. During running-in period, despite the scratches and defects formed on Si3N4 wear surface, NH2-SiO2 nanoparticles were attached to the wear surface (Fig. 5e), that were beneficial for forming smooth surfaces and protecting the surface from wear. After the running-in period, NH2-SiO2 particles gradually deposited on the worn surface; thereinto, a portion of the particles were visible, while others were obscured by a layer and consequently became blurred. This was mainly attributed to the surface film generated by NH2-SiO2 cooperating with silica gel derived from tribochemical reactions (Fig. 5f). Then, partial surface film peeled off and new film formed at the friction interface in succession, thus, achieving a dynamic balance and maintaining the ultra-low friction.

In summary, extensive works have certified that 0D-nanoadditives could be considered as useful candidates for solid/liquid systems to achieve outstanding lubrication, even for superlubricity. Friction and wear behaviors of solid composites primarily depend on the chemical properties of solid matrix, dispersion and uniformity of additives, the interface bonding strength with their matrixes, and the formation of a tribo-film. For metal or ceramic substrates, 0D-nanoparticles provide the grain refinement and defect repair, thus endowing the composite system with improved mechanical and tribological properties. Similarly, 0D-materials can also enhance the mechanical properties of polymers, but the optimization mechanism is derived from its nucleation ability. For liquid lubrication systems, the rolling effect and surface repair of 0D-additives, and the lubricant viscosity play the dominant roles in the tribological behaviors. Moreover, the friction conversion from sliding to rolling, as well as the tribo-film formation via chemical reactions and physical mechanisms on worn-surfaces, excellently contribute to the improvement of tribological properties. As the additive dimension changes from 0D to 1D, nanoadditives on the worn surface render the contact modes of tribo-pairs to be transformed from point-contact into line-contact. The distinct toughening mechanism and rolling along the axis of the 1D-additives are responsible for low frictional resistance and excellent antiwear ability of solid/liquid lubrication systems.

With resemblance to 0D-nanoadditives, 1D-nanomaterials are also being increasingly regarded as excellent lubricating additives for the wide ranges of tribological applications, due to their unique nano-rolling and toughening mechanisms. Plenty of research works have been done to develop the remarkable tribological properties for the composites reinforced by 1D-nanoadditives [144–146]. More details in arresting improvements of tribological performances for 1D nanoadditive-related materials have been presented in Table 2 . This section mainly discusses the friction behaviors and wear mechanisms of 1D-nanoadditives for solid and liquid lubrication systems; next, their contributions for superlubricity are also discussed.

Metal-based composites: For 1D-metal-based composites, the crucial parameters for controlling the friction and wear behaviors are the dispersion uniformity and stability of nanomaterials. Akin to 0D-composites, successful synthesis of metal-based materials containing well-dispersed 1D-nanoadditive remains a challenge. The SiC particles were applied as the secondary dispersion agent to improve dispersible homogeneity of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) in Al-6061 matrix [147]. Stronger hardness and lower wear rate of the as-prepared complex were observed compared to individual CNT, because of CNTs/SiC distribution. Meanwhile, the well-distributed nanoadditives resulted in an expected improvement in microstructure that was also critical to tribological performance [148,149]. Ni-coated single-walled CNT (Ni-SWCNT) was ensured to enhance the tribological properties of Cu-substrate composites [150]. Interfacial bonding strength was improved prominently through Ni-treatment, which contributed to the improving microstructure of composite. Thus, Ni-SWNT/Cu composites showed excellent mechanical and tribological properties.

It was remarkable that, friction and wear of metal-based materials occurred through the wear-induced surface deformation, destruction, or reactions [151]. At the low loads, well-aligned 1D-nanoadditives played a crucial role in suppressing material deformation due to rolling mechanism [152]; under high loads, the uniformly dispersed nanoadditives were responsible for the formation of high-performance tribofilms. The films exhibited low shear strength and provided a feasible strategy to prevent direct collision of tribo-pairs [153–155]. Double-walled CNT/Cu (DWCNT/Cu) composites exhibited lower wear rates and smaller friction coefficients than those of pure Cu [156]. The detailed values of wear rates and friction coefficients have been presented in Table 2. Deformation and breaking of the surface DWCNTs and their bundles during friction resulted in the formation of a graphitized lubricating tribofilm in the contact [157], that contributed to achieving low friction and wear for such composites.

Polymer-based composites: 1D-nanomaterials reinforced polymer exhibited high load-bearing capacity [158], and gained increasing attention for their friction and wear behaviors. Shearing lag models for fiber-reinforced composites have been proven to be important for nanoadditive-reinforced composites [159]. Surface stress was transferred to the nanoadditives (

), 1D-nanoadditives performed larger load-transfer ability and interfacial bonding with their solid matrices.

1D-carbon fiber is considered as a new-type carbonaceous material, which generally contains greater than 92% carbon and exhibits high strength, high modulus, low density, excellent chemical stability, as well as the high-temperature self-lubricating effect [161]. These features render such filler to be promising candidate for improving tribological performances of the polymer composites. Specifically, due to their excellent modulus and strength, carbon fibers can bear a large proportion of loads between the tribo-contacting interfaces during sliding, thus caused the improved wear-resistant capacity of polymer composite to be obtained [162,163]. Compared to the micro-scale carbon fibers, carbon nanofibers (CNFs) own higher aspect ratios (>200) and larger specific surface areas; this enables them to interact strongly with other components in composites, results in better interfacial bonding for the efficient load-transferring, further contributing to improved friction reduction and anti-wear properties [32,164,165]. In addition, small size of the CNF debris will minimize the roughness of two contacting surfaces, possibly exhibiting better lubricating ability [166].

Contrastive experiments of micro-sized carbon fibers (CMFs) and CNFs on the friction and wear performances of high-strength glass fabric (HSGF)/phenolic laminates were reported [167]. When the incorporated CNFs (1.0–3.0 wt%), interfacial adhesion between the HSGF and phenolic matrix was improved, in consequence, the CNFs/HSGF/phenolic exhibited good load-transfer ability, anti-friction and wear-resistant actions; moreover, in the case of 4.0 wt% CNFs, the coefficient of friction was reduced to about 0.1, while the wear rate was increased due to the poor interface caused by CNF aggregation [168]. For CMFs/HSGF/phenolic, although CMFs (1.0–4.0 wt%) caused the interfacial adhesion to be worsened, owing to the lack of attractive nano-characters and active groups at the CMF surface; its friction coefficient was as low as 0.15 and the wear rates were reduced in succession. In particular, 4.0 wt%-CMFs addition to HSGF/phenolic led to significant reductions of almost two orders of magnitude in the wear rate, compared with that of raw HSGF/phenolic laminate, which was attributed to the higher load-capability of CMFs. In short, the CNFs were of better effects in improving the friction-reduction characteristic of HSGF/phenolic laminate than that of CMFs; whereas CMFs play more outstanding role in providing higher anti-wear behavior compared to CNFs.

Compared with the low aspect ratio carbon nanotubes (LARC), high aspect ratio carbon nanotubes (HARC) had the similar morphology with polymer chains, resulted in stronger interfacial bonds between HARC and polymer [169]. Strong bonding of the HARCs combined with polymer chains and their self-entangled compacted structures, prevented the wear debris to be removed and the relative motion of chains. Therefore, the marked reductions of friction coefficients and wear rates appeared during wear. However, for nanomaterials mixed with polymers, the relatively low interfacial interactions between 1D-nanoadditives and the matrixes still remained open to researchers [170,171] Neat poly (vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) exhibited three different crystalline phases (α, β, and γ) relying on the chain conformation with the trans and gauche linkages. The α-phase was preferred for tribological applications due to its non-polar configuration decreased the induced dipole-dipole moments between PVDF chains during friction [172,173]. Lee et al. [174] prepared carbon nanorod (CNR) filler-embedded PVDF composite. Synthetic schematic of the PVDF/CNR composite was presented in Fig. 6a . With the incorporation of CNRs, the configuration of the PVDF chain structure changed from the β-phase to α-phase because of the interaction between PVDF polymer chains with respect to the CNRs, resulted in remarkable improvements in tribological behaviors and interfacial adhesion properties of the composites. Table 2 provided the friction and wear results. Compared to neat PVDF, the reductions occurred in friction coefficients from 0.43 to 0.03 and wear rates from 8.43×10−5 to 3.70×10−5 mm3/Nm were attested to be better for the PVDF/10 wt%-CNR composites.

Additionally, the silicon carbide nanowires (SiCnws) have been demonstrated to be the ideal candidates for further optimizing the tribological behaviors of carbon fiber reinforced resin matrix composites (CFRRMC). Tribological comparisons of pyrolytic carbon modified CFRRCM (PyCCFRRCM) and pyrolytic carbon/SiCnws modified CFRRCM (PyC/SiCnws-CFRRCM) have been carried out [31]. After wear tests, the distinctive features of microstructure for as-prepared samples were presented in Fig. 6b–d. Different from other samples, a prominent tribofilm formed on the wear surface of PyC/SiCnws-CFRRCM, and smoothed the contacted surfaces. High-magnification morphologies of the tribofilm for SiCnws-CFRRMC in Fig. 6e and F have been analyzed in detail. Further energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis about points 1 and 2 indicated the presence of Si element (Fig. 6 g). As a result, it inferred that SiCnws was mixed with resin, and widely distributed on the surfaces of carbon fibers (CFs). Aforesaid results explained the existence of SiCnws on the worn surface of the SiCnws-CFRRMC during friction. It was noteworthy that, the bridging effect of SiCnws rendered the resin to be well distributed onto the CF surface, that strongly enhanced the interface bonding between the resin and CFs, thus preventing the severe damage (such as fiber breakage and resin shattering) and improving tribological properties.

Ceramic-based composites: Based on the superior natures of ceramics for lightness, thermal insulation and high hardness, many researches have focused on developing the potential of ceramic-base composites [175]. Unfortunately, the brittleness of hard ceramics limits their tribological potentials. In this context, 1D-nanoadditives offer an attractive strategy for improving ceramic toughness [176,177]. Remarkable increase in the toughness of ceramic/1D-nanomaterial composites is mainly caused by 1D-materials serving as “ligaments” between the ceramic grains: 1D-nanomaterials embedded in grain boundaries progressively uncoil, unfurl, and stretch in the crack's wake as the crack propagates intergranularly, bridging the crack until they eventually fail far behind the crack tip.

CNTs performed a dominant role in wear behaviors of SiC ceramics. Investigation results have shown that SiC/CNTs composite possessed better wear resistance than that of SiC-ceramic [178]. Concretely, smaller worn volumes and the lower steady-state wear were observed. Superior wear resistance of the SiC/CNTs nanocomposite was closely related to synergistic effects of SiC/CNTs lubrication and toughening functions provided by CNTs. The former endowed the sliding-contact with a mild wear; the latter hindering the propagation and coalescence of the short cracks caused by grain pull-out.

Alexander et al. [159] reported the tribological behaviors of short carbon fibers (Cf) and CNTs acted as boron carbide (B4C) reinforcements. The huge differences in static friction coefficients were observed in B4CCNTs (0.27) and B4CCf (0.13). In contrast, the dynamic friction coefficients were closed to each other, approximately 0.11, as shown in Table 2. Lower friction coefficients were ascribed to the lubrication abilities of CNTs and Cf. However, it remained an impediment to disperse Cf in the matrix, because their clusters were related with residual porosities of the matrix. The higher wear rates were observed in B4CCf due to their agglomeration of the Cf, if compared to those of B4CCNTs, despite of similar friction coefficients.

Above-mentioned discussions reveal that uniform dispersion of 1D-nanoadditives provides excellent tribological optimization regardless of the substrate. For metal matrix composites, under high loads, successful formation of tribo-film containing nanoadditives effectively inhibits the collision between tribo-pairs, thus improving the anti-wear ability of composites. Meanwhile, 1D-nanomaterials with the linear structure provide attractive toughening effect for ceramic matrix, that is helpful for optimizing the wear resistance ability. Regarding polymer-based composites, 1D-materials with a high aspect ratio exhibit expected interfacial compatibility with polymer matrix due to the similar morphology to the polymer chain, result in the formation of self-entangled compact structure of composites, thus inhibiting the removal of wear debris and the relative movement of polymer chains.

In analogy to nanoadditive-enhanced solid materials, the homogenous distributions of 1D-nanoadditives in the fluid matrices are also critical for realizing their full potentials in liquid mixed lubrication. Improvements in tribological performances of the lubricants containing 1D-nanoadditives have been increasingly recognized. However, this benefited lubrication is short-lived, because of the self-induced reaggregation of nanoadditives in lubricants [41]. Therefore, the first issue is importantly focused on increasing the dispersion stability of nanometer additives.

Concerning the 1D-liquid lubrication system, the stability and lubrication behavior strongly depend on surface characteristics of the nanoadditives. Surface modification technology aims to reduce the surface tension of materials and change their surface polarity. Through intermolecular forces (such as van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonds, etc.), the modifiers are attached to the nanoadditives’ surface, and forming the coatings. For instance, oleic acid changed the surface of CuS nanorods from hydrophobic to hydrophilic, and improved their dispersity in liquid paraffin (LP) [179]. Tribological experiments indicated that the friction reduction and anti-wear abilities of the modified-CuS were better than pure CuS nanorods, due to their stable dispersion in the lubricating oil, the specific test conditions and tribological performances have been provided in Table 2. After testing at a rotating speed of 300 r/min and the normal force of 300 N, the wear rate of 3.05 × 10−8 m3/Nm and friction coefficient for 0.0345 were observed, respectively. Furthermore, synergistic effects of CNT/SiO2 nanofluids have also attracted special attention [180]. As presented in Fig. 7a , CNT defects could easily react with H2O molecules or O2 in the atmosphere, and thus induced appearance of -C=O on the CNT surface. The -C=O based triboechemical reaction film strongly adsorbed on the worn surface, which was essential for an efficient long-term lubrication. However, excess CNTs in the nanofluids tended to aggregate owing to their poor dispersion in the water. Hence, nano-SiO2 served as a dispersant at the suitable concentration, providing an effective strategy for CNT/SiO2 combined nanofluid to achieve stable and excellent lubrication.

Further, the introduction of functional additives (such as anti-wear (AW), extreme pressure (EP), detergents and dispersants) into 1D nanoadditive-containing lubricants has been considered as an effective approach for improving tribological performances [181,182]. A comprehensive exploration about the influence of functional oil additives on the tribological performances of lubricant mixtures containing MoS2 nanotubes (NTs) has been conducted [182]. As shown in Fig. 7b–e, the similar friction and wear performances of MoS2 NTs were ensured under mixed rolling/sliding, indicating the prominent lubrication performances were independent with the accompanying additives. However, under reciprocating sliding, the interactions between the MoS2 NTs and the functional additives emerged the apparent discrepancy. Only AW and detergent additives provided the excellent cooperations with MoS2 NTs, as seen from Fig. 7c and d. The antagonism between the MoS2 NTs and the dispersant was certified to be particularly remarkable, that has been indicated in Fig. 7e. Approximately 11% improvement in the friction for MoS2 NTs/dispersant system was discovered under reciprocating sliding conditions. Inversely, in mixed rolling/sliding process, addition of dispersants into polyolefin (PAO) oil improved the frictional behaviors slightly (Fig. 7b and e); the NTs in the blend made tribological performances be further improved up to 24%. It was obvious that significant optimization of the dispersant was helpful in enhancing nano-additive dispersion stability; however, the improvement effects on dispersion stability were likely limited by the rolling or sliding conditions.

From these above-mentioned works, it was concluded that surface modifications and low concentration of CNTs, and the use of functional additives provide a versatile aisle for achieving good dispersion stability of CNTs, thereby promoting the further improvements of tribological behaviors of liquid-lubricated systems.

Nanoadditives also promoted the tribofilm formation, reduced the frictional resistance of contact interfaces, and consequently affected tribological behaviors of the lubricants. The 3 wt%-TiO2/CNTs rendered the friction coefficients of nano-grease to be reduced by 60%, in comparison with raw calcium grease [183]. Nanoadditives induced the formation of a tribo-film at the tribo-interfaces during wear, and acted as the useful separation layer to avoid contact surface asperities. Similar contributions about 1D MoS2-nanotubes [184] or MWCNTs [185] induced tribofilms have also been well reported, for purpose of improvements in the tribological performances of PAO oil or ionic liquids (ILs) liquid lubrication systems, respectively. Thereinto, it was noteworthy that [185], 1D-MWCNTs when compared to 2D-graphene were more effective in reducing the friction coefficients and wear rates of ILs, at low loads; inversely, graphene effect was better at high loads (Table 2). However, taking into account the repeated friction process, 1D-nanoadditives with appropriate dimensions in lubricants can be filled into surface defects of the rubbing surface and provide the nano-rolling effect, further attaining the signally enhanced tribological behaviors [185,186]. Such as, Ye et al. [186] discussed the tribological properties of MWCNTs with mutative diameters and lengths in the fluid lubricants. Approximately diameters (10–20 nm) and lengths (0.5–2 μm) rendered the MWCNTs to readily enter the clearance of friction pairs, played the role of sliding and rolling between friction pairs, and filled into the cracks to repair surface damages; thus, the enhancements in tribological behaviors were obtained.

Regarding 1D nanoadditive-containing lubrication systems, the key to tribological optimization is the dispersion stability of nanomaterials. On the basis of stable dispersion, the surface electrostatic potential results in the adsorption of additives to the friction surface, repairing the friction surface, and leading to the formation of physical adsorption film; subsequently, with the assistance of high pressure, frictional heat and mechanical energy, the adsorption film begins to decompose and induce tribochemical reactions, resulting in the formation of tribochemical film that is directly responsible for improved tribological behavior of lubrication system.

Coaxial cylindrical multi-walled CNTs with high aspect ratios, can be regarded as ideal materials for achieving the superlubricity. Lately, remarkable progress of macroscale superlubricity for 1D-nanomaterial is achieved from the CNTs. Defect-free double-walled CNTs became the “smoothest bearings” [46]. In the case of incommensurate contact, the friction and wear nearly vanish because of the suppression of stick-slip motions [187]. The inter-shell friction of double-walled CNTs (DWCNTs) has been measured using an inner-shell pull-out tester, under ambient conditions [188], as shown in Fig. 8a . Fig. 8b–e provided transmission electron microscopy (TEM) morphologies of DWCNT. According to the measured results, the frictional force between the nanotube shells was lower than 1 nN, and was independent with nanotube length. Shear strength of the DWCNTs was only several pascals, the four orders of magnitude reduction was observed than the reported value of the CNTs [189]. In addition to the incommensurate effect, the defect absence of CNTs has unenergetically preferred positions for both inner and outer shells, and played the significant role in the origins of superlubricity.

Apart from an incommensurate contact, the wear-induced evolution of composite structure also played an important role in achieving the macroscopic superlubric state with the durable and stable behaviors. The graphite-like carbon, fullerene-like carbon and amorphous carbon films containing the curved graphene enabled an occurrence of superlubricity in the unique way. The super-low friction has been obtained by constituting graphite-like carbon and fullerene-like carbon films [190]. Such superlubricated state was mainly dominated by the formation of 1D-structural graphene nanoscrolls (GNSs) at the sliding interface, which decreased the direct contact areas, resulting in significant reduction in the friction coefficients (∼0.005). Furthermore, the formation of friction film on the contact surface is also responsible for a ultra-low friction [191]. Gong et al. [192] specifically reported the superlubricating behavior of tribofilm. From the microstructure of tribofilm debris, it clearly observed that graphene coiled into annular or tubular structures to largely coat on the amorphous cores, and formed GNSs. For the surface aggregation of GNSs, the friction coefficients accordingly decreased. The growth model for the GNSs and friction mechanism transformation of a-C materials were correspondingly provided in Fig. 8f and g. An incommensurate contact between friction surfaces and the GNSs allowed the dual surface to overcome the relatively small energetic barriers, thereby achieving super-low friction.

In summary, 1D-nanoadditives have gained particular attentions for their demonstrated tribological potentials. Basic lubrication mechanisms have been extensively proposed. In addition to aforementioned dispersion state of nanoadditives and the interfacial bonding strength; in solid lubricating systems, grain refinement effect, toughening action, nano-rolling, and tribofilm mechanism are also considered to be the key factors governing the friction and wear behaviors of nanocomposites. In addition, additives adsorbed to friction surfaces provide a feasible pathway to repair surface defects, and induce the formation of high-performance chemical reaction films in liquid lubrication systems. These physical and chemical phenomena are responsible for the optimization of lubrication ability. Besides the large-scaled use in tribology-related fields, the unique structure and physicochemical performances of 1D-nanomaterials make them potential candidates for broad applications in other fields, such as photocatalysts, photodetectors, solar cells, gas sensors, biosensors, and batteries, etc. It is remarkable that, with the altered nanostructure from 1D to 2D, the layered characteristic attaches the easy-slipped interlaminar behavior to the multilayer 2D-nanoadditives, making them increasingly promising candidates for friction reduction and wear optimization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antiwear enhancement and friction decline of 2D-nanoadditives
ce:section.ce:para: 2D-nanoadditives possess remarkable performances, such as high mechanical strength, thermal stability, low surface energy and atomic smooth surface, that render them to be promising solid lubricants at micro/macro-scaled tribological applications [51,196,197]. Besides that, the superlubricity of 2D-materials with the extremely low coefficient of friction (less than 0.01) has been increasing recognized by forming ideal incommensurate contact [198]. This section mainly introduces the recent progress of 2D-nanoadditives in solid and liquid lubricating systems, the more details have been explored and recorded in the Table 3 . So far, important achievements and main mechanisms of 2D-nanomaterial superlubricity are the concerned topics.

Considering the increasingly sophisticated techniques in the synthesis of composites, massive researches have attested promising potential of well-dispersed nanoadditives in significantly improving the mechanical and tribological behaviors of nanocomposites [199,200]. In particular, the tribo-systems containing graphene and its derivatives [201], MoS2 [197,202], as well as 2D-MXenes (2D transition metal carbides, nitrides, and carbonitrides) [203,204], exhibit outstanding friction and wear behaviors, making them an attractive for improving the efficiency, durability and environmental compatibility of future mechanical systems.

Metal-based composites: A well-dispersion of 2D-nanoadditive in solid matrix for fabricating expected metal-based composites has been studied extensively [205]. An important work indicated that, well-dispersed graphene improved the friction and wear performances of a AlSi10Mg-matrix [200], which was ascribed to the self-lubricating behaviors of 2D-materials, making them be prone to form interlayered sliding, further descending friction effectively. Similarly, in our groups’ woks on TiAl-SnAgCu/graphene composites [206], the friction and wear improvements were not only attributed to the self-lubrication effect of 2D graphene, but also the improved hardness and the composite.

Recently, the ab initio calculation is increasingly being used to evaluate tribological performances of advanced functional material systems. The ab initio calculation can be divided into two categories, namely, the density function theory (DFT) methods and wave-function-based methods. For important works of 2D-nanomaterials with extended lattice structures, DFT method is used more often than the wave-function-based method, resulting in the former being commonly regarded as equivalent to ab initio calculation [135,207,208]. Considering the humidity influences on tribological properties of the head-disk interface in magnetic data storage devices, in a relevant work, Broitman et al. [208] studied the water uptake abilities of amorphous carbon nitride (a-CNx) films, fullerene-like carbon nitride (FL-CNx), and amorphous carbon (a-C) films. Among of which, the number of adsorbed water in pure a-C films was highest; moreover, FL-CNx films adsorbed less than a-CNx. Low water uptake of FL-CNx film was likely related to the lack of dangling bonds on its surface. Subsequent first-principles calculations in DFT framework indicated that, higher energy cost of the various dangling bonds in different configurations was found into FL-CNx compared with amorphous films, thus, such dangling bonds were much less likely to occur in FL-CNx than in a-CNx and a-C films. Overall, the demonstrated superior tribological properties and low water adsorption of FL-CNx films would make them impart a huge potential for their applications of hard-disk with metal interfaces.

Additionally, Berman et al. [209] were the first group to report the macroscopic frictional behavior and wear resistance of one atom thick graphene coating. When tested in the hydrogen condition, monolayer graphene on the steel substrate could last for 6.4 × 103 sliding cycles, with a low and steady friction coefficient (∼0.26) between two sliding steel interfaces. Unlike in graphene, graphene oxide (GO) is a distorted structure of sp2-carbon nanosheets [210]. Due to the oxygen functionalities, numerous sp3-carbons are infused into the honeycomb lattice structures [211]. Nano-structure of highly hydrophilic oxygen-rich functional groups (hydroxide, carboxyl, carbonyl, etc.) on the GO surface endowed it with superior adhesion to the metal matrix, making it an attractive candidate for solid lubricant. In both Ni-based coatings [212] and Co-based coatings [213], GO showed the excellent friction-reducing and anti-wear effects. A lubrication mechanism included that presence of the GO refined coating structures, improved their mechanical properties (like hardness, toughness, etc.), and promoted the tribo-film to be formed at the contact interface, thus avoiding direct contacts between tribo-pairs.

MXene nanosheets have particularly received interests for tribological purposes due to their graphite-like structures, low shear strength and potential self-lubrication abilities [214,215]. Tunable mechanical properties arising from their structural diversity are responsible for their abilities to compete with state-of-the-art 2D-nanoadditives such as graphene in terms of tribological performances [128,216,217]. Homogeneous distributions of 2D-Ti3C2Tx have been verified to be responsible for the improved tribological behaviors of Ti3C2Tx/Al [218]. Additionally, the effects of contact pressure and relative humidity on friction and wear behaviors of MXenes’ coatings in high loaded steel/steel dry sliding contacts [219]. The authors speculated that MXene-coated samples exhibited a 2.3-time reduction in friction and the 2.7-fold decrease in wear volume under moderate contact pressure (0.80 GPa) and low relative humidity (20%), as compared to the uncoated steel. This was due to the generation of a tribo-film composed of Ti3C2Tx MXene. Conversely, excessive pressure caused the wear-protecting friction layer to be partially ruptured, that reduced its beneficial effects. Motivated by this, Marian et al. further explored the friction reduction and anti-wear properties of MXenes’ coatings in the case of higher loaded rolling-sliding contacts in thrust ball bearings under ambient conditions. It has been believed that the decreases of about 3.2 in frictional torque and almost 2.9 in linear cumulative wear rate were attained, while extending the service life of testing component by approximately 2.1 times, in contrast to uncoated sample [220]. Notably, when recently compared to reports on MoS2 and diamond-like carbon coatings, the Ti3C2Tx MXene coating significantly extend the service life of bearing parts by ∼30% and ∼55%, in spite of their similar frictional torques during the steady-operation [221].

Overall, based on above-mentioned reports, in addition to the service conditions and reinforcement contents, the presence of tribo-layers played the crucial role in improving tribological properties. The tribo-layer may consist of a single-layer, double-layer, or multi-layer structure that exists on the surface of a grain-refined layer or a plastically deformed layer in the substrate. In general, tribo-layer with a single-layer structure, also called the tribofilm or lubricating film, and has been proven to be effective for the reduction of frictional resistance and increasing wear resistance [219]. Recently, research showed that the frictional interface of multilayer graphene/Fe2O3-enhanced titanium alloy formed the beneficial tribo-layer composing of multilayer graphene layer and Fe2O3 layer; such tribo-layer with load-bearing and lubricant capacity, coupled with the synergistic effect between graphene layer and Fe2O3 layer were responsible for reducing the friction and wear [222]. Furthermore, in our group's previous work [223], the titanium alloy reinforced with MgAl-graphene-Al2O3 owned outstanding anti-friction and wear resistant abilities; this was explained by the generation of a multilayer-structure tribo-layer mainly comprising of the Ti-alloy, MgAl-Al2O3, and graphene layers. Interlamination separation of the multilayer graphene along with Al2O3 rolling induced the observable improvement of MgAl deformation. This resulted in a self-recovery effect of the tribo-interface, thus, the self-regulation capacity of MgAl-graphene-Al2O3 was well improved, ensuring that the Ti-MgAl-graphene-Al2O3 composites achieved the excellent friction reduction and anti-wear performances.

Polymer-based composites: For the polymer matrix, the interfacial strength of reinforcement combined with the matrix is also an important factor in determining the mechanical and tribological properties of this solid composite system [224,225]. Yang et al. [199] incorporated polydopamine modified reduced GO (D-rGO) into PTFE/Nomex fabric (D-fabric) composites for improving their tribological performances. The bonding strength of d-rGO with their matrixes significantly improved after functionalization using the polydopamine. Wear testing results revealed that d-rGO reinforced d-fabric phenolic composites exhibited the excellent friction and wear behaviors. Li et al. [225] synthesized the solvent-free GO nanoribbons colloids (GONRs-M2070) to evaluate their tribological performances as epoxy (EP) additives. Mean wear rate of the 0.6 wt%-GONRs-M2070/EP composite was acquired as 7.65×10−5 g/min, that decreased by 62.7% compared to that (2.05×10−4 g/min) of plain EP.

In addition to serving as reinforcements, 2D-nanomaterials are also considered as attractive modified candidates. MoS2-nanosheets covered p-phenylene benzobisoxazole (PBO) fibers were applied in thermoplastic polyimide/polyetheretherketone (TPI/PEEK) to improve its friction and wear behaviors; typical schematic of the frictional test equipment was shown in Fig. 9a
[226]. The interfacial adhesion of the blended matrix with PBO fiber was improved by decorating MoS2 onto PBO. Compared to their matrices, load carrying capacity and anti-abrasion of the composites were greater under 200 °C. Macroscopical friction coefficients and wear rates were reduced by 22.9% and 61.1%, respectively. Fig. 9b–e demonstrated that PBO-MoS2 hybrids appeared slowly at first, and a lubricating film was then generated between counterpart and matrix under the action of repeated stresses. As can be seen from Fig. 9b–e, this lubricating film contained a large amount of MoS2. Since the MoS2 was easy-to-shear, it endowed the film with excellent lubrication capacity, which effectively reduced frictional resistance. With MoS2 tribo-film formation, direct contact of the polymer with respect to counterpart was further lessened, consequently protecting the substrate from wear damage. It was remarkable that the polymer-based composites containing PBO-MoS2 additive was found to exhibit better anti-wear performances, especially under the elevated temperatures, if compared with the raw PBO fiber-enhanced composites. In addition, TGA analysis further indicated the superior thermal stability of the PBO-MoS2 reinforced TPI/PEEK composite, that was of low degradation rate during the sliding process.

Unique molecular structures and significant lubrication functions of 2D-nanoadditives play the versatile roles in the remarkable adjustment of tribological performances for solid lubrication systems [4,7]. High strength between the atoms of single layer and extremely low shear-strength in interlamination make them advantageous candidates for the rubbing surface lubrication [41]. Inspired by this, multilayer graphene/poly (vinyl chloride) (MLG/PVC) composites have been successfully synthesized [227]. Compared to neat PVC, synergistic actions between the enhanced toughness of MLG/PVC and excellent self-lubrication of MLG resulted in a 56% reduction for the wear rates of composites, as shown in Table 3. In addition, the roles of GO on tribological behaviors of ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene (UHMWPE) have been explored specially [228].
