Acquired results showed the GO-composites possessed lower friction and higher wear resistance, as compared with unfilled UHMWPE; it mainly ascribed to the lubricating actions of layered GO under sliding contacts.

Ceramic-based composites: Numerous investigators have devoted to the improvements of hardness and fracture toughness ceramics, which is crucial in obtaining a reliable wear-resistance composite [229–231]. Ranjan et al. [232] fabricated graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs) to reinforce TiN-coating on the Ti alloys. 2 wt.%-GNPs rendered the coatings to acquire such improvements of 19% in hardness and of 300% in fracture toughness. These enhancements resulted in tribological behaviors of GNPs/TiN being superior to those of TiN coating. Furthermore, explorations on the tribological behaviors of 1 wt.% GNPs-reinforced Al2O3 composite have been performed [233]. Sliding wear tests revealed a two order of magnitude reduction in specific wear rate (from ∼105 to ∼107 mm3/Nm) of Al2O3/GNP composite, as compared to unreinforced Al2O3. A proposed explanation for outstanding wear ability of composite ensured that GNP showed an effective pathway to improve fracture toughness and bearing capacity of the composites.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations revealed the strong relationship of the fracture toughness of ceramic composites with respect to dimensions, volume fractions, and layer counts for 2D-nanoadditives [234]. In the case of a given volume fraction and additive size, the fewer layered (1 or 2 layers) sheet-like additives contributed to forming more bridges, resulting in the attractive bridging toughening effects for ceramics. As the additive sizes and volume fractions increased, large amounts of flake additives effectively inhibited the crack propagation, and endowed the ceramic composites with increased fracture toughness. However, overhigh contents resulted in composites with poor friction and wear properties due to additive self-agglomeration [235]. Appropriate concentrations and dimensions of 2D-nanofillers were expected to account for the increased toughness and coating application interests.

For 2D-additives reinforced solid lubrication systems, their unique molecular structure and significant lubrication function perform an important role in the tribological properties. High interatomic strength of monolayer nanosheet provides a feasible strategy for improving mechanical properties (hardness and toughness) of the matrix. Extremely low interlayer shear strength of the multilayer structure endows the nanoadditive with excellent self-lubricating ability that is crucial to the excellent tribological behavior of solid composites.

Massive experimental works have reported that multilayer 2D-nanoadditives are well-bonded by weak Van-Der-Waals force, and obtain relatively low shear strength, thus, adjacent layers are easy-sheared to achieve active load transfer under extreme pressures, making them promising candidates for solid-liquid mixed lubrication enhancements [47,236]. Thanks to particular structural characteristics, a small amount of 2D-nanoadditives in lubricants can not only effectively reduce the friction coefficient, but also endow tribo-pairs with a significant improvement in anti-wear performance [237].

Friction-induced interlayer slip is directly responsible for the reduced frictional resistance due to the low shear strength of 2D-nanoadditives; these 2D-materials in the lubricants are prone to enter the contact area to fill the concave holes, thereby repairing the contact surface defects and enhancing the anti-wear abilities of contact interfaces [238–241]. Very recently, Liñeira del Río and coworkers explored the impacts of GNPs as additives on the tribological behaviors of polyalphaolefin neat oil (PAO40). It indicated that adding of 0.5 wt% into PAO40 nanolubricants led to a 21% reduction in the friction and a 22% decrease in wear track width [240]. Another published work verified that compared with reference oil (5W-30), the compound nano-lubricants containing graphene markedly improved the anti-friction/wear capacities (29–35%/22–29%) of automobile engine worn surfaces [241]. Based on aforementioned works, important effects regarding the superior friction reduction and wear resistant capacities, were mainly attributed the surface repairing and tribofilm generation from the nanoadditives [240,241]. Comparing to graphene, GO exhibits a large number of oxygenated-functional groups onto the basal plane and its edges (hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups), consequently providing further opportunity for improving liquid lubrication [7]. Study of Wu et al. indicated that the anti-friction property, wear resistance, and load-carrying capacity of lubricants containing 0.5 wt% GO were increased by 14.7%, 34%, and 18.2%, respectively [242], as opposed to the base oil. The tiny layered-structure of GO enabled it to easily enter the contact interfaces and subsequently adhere to the tribo-surfaces, thus, forming a tribofilm to prevent tribo-pairs from contacting directly and material transfer.

From the aforementioned 2D nanoadditive-related studies, it is concluded that the formation of a tribo-film containing additive is crucial for improvements of friction and wear performances of the lubricating systems [243,244]. In this regard, Wen et al. [245] fabricated several graphene-like covalent-organic frameworks (GCF) nanosheets, and systematically compared their tribological performances with GO, and rGO in PAO10 oils. Fig. 10a was the fabrication diagrams of GCF, and the evolution reactions at different stages were shown in Fig. 10b–F. Main lubrication models of GCF, rGO and GO dispersed oils in Fig. 10 g showed that conjugated systems of GCF brought p-d feedback coordination to metal ions created by exoelectron, promoted the formation of adsorbed film to improve the stability. Mechanical strength of covalent bonding structure in-plane ensured carrying capacity and the durability; the ultrathin layer features declined shear stress with slide effects. Eventually, the excellent reductions in anti-friction (more than 53.5%) and wear-resistant (about 95.4%) were ideally achieved at the ultra-low dosage of approximatively 0.008 wt%. The observed dissociation of CO linkages of rGO and GO induced by the tribostress and frictional heats, deteriorating the stability and durability of tribofilm, resulted in the depravation of tribological behaviors in the mixed PAO10 oils.

Despite excellent friction features of 2D-nanoadditives, the limited dispersion-stability in base oil and weak adhesion to their substrates, caused by the characteristic “puckering effect” of 2D-layer sheets, still remain the great obstacle for a wide range of tribological applications [211,246]. So far, crucial strategies from chemical modification to alkyl chains grafting, subsequently to oleophilic surfactant assistance, have been developed to implement the full potential of nanoadditives in tribology. Tribological properties of the oleic acid-modified lanthanum trifluoride-GO (OA-LaF3-GO) nanohybrids as lubricant additives in liquid paraffin have been evaluated [247]. Presence of the oleic acid led to an excellent dispersion stability of OA-LaF3-GO nanohybrids in liquid paraffin. This has rendered that the stable suspension was easily transferred onto the contact zones of rubbing steels and deposited thereon, preventing the rough surfaces from coming into direct contact; thus, lubrication behaviors of liquid paraffin were enhanced visibly. Ni-treated MoS2-nanosheets exhibited excellent friction and wear behaviors as the SN 500 mineral oils additives [237]. After four-ball wear testing under a 1200 rpm speed and the load of 392.4 N, 0.5 wt%-Ni-MoS2 endowed the compound oils with the noteworthy enhancement in anti-wear property (almost 40–50%) and slightly descended friction coefficients (approximately 15–20%); moreover, more important details on the friction and wear measures have been listed in Table 3.

Additionally, Liu et al. [248] synthesized PEI-rGO with observable homogeneous distributions in the water (see Fig. 11a ), and it demonstrated the significant reductions in frictional coefficients (54.6%) and wear rates (45.0%) by applying 0.05 wt% PEI-rGO into water, as compared to the pure deionized water. Min et al. [249] executed the crucial experiments to contrast tribological performances of fluorinated GO (FGO) and fluorinated rGO (FrGO) served as water-based additives. Adding 0.7 wt% FGO into base-water achieved 41.4% decrease in friction coefficients and 88.1% reduction in wear rates (Fig. 11b and c). Furthermore, FGO-0.7 wt% additives presented the more effective ways to improve the tribological performances under chosen working conditions, compared with the GO-0.7 wt% and FrGO-0.7 wt%. Based on the frictional mechanisms of water-based lubricant shown in Fig. 11d, FrGO deposited in the gully worn surface resulting from its poor dispersibility in water. Difference from FrGO was that FGO surfaces contained much of hydrophilic oxygenated-functional groups, that availably dispersed them uniformly in water, thereby facilitating the lubrication capability.

Similar to 0D and 1D nanoadditives, the 2D-nanoadditives in their lubricants are prone to enter the contact area due to the super-small size, providing a viable strategy for repairing worn surface defects. At the same time, due to the extremely low interlayer shear strength of multi-layer nanoadditives, the adjacent layer shear dominates the load-transferring under extreme pressure. Tribofilm containing nanoadditives exhibits excellent carrying-capacity and durability, thus helping in the tribological optimization of the lubrication system. However, the "puckering effect" of 2D-additives reduces their dispersion stability and inhibits their surface repair and tribological optimization effects. Further research should be focused on developing effective strategies to suppress this phenomenon.

To date, 2D-nanoadditives have received particular attentions for their demonstrated potential superlubricity [45,198,250]. Regarding the homogeneous 2D-materials, the lattice commensurability between two contacting surfaces is of great significance for their tribological behaviors. Feng et al. [251] performed a pivotal investigation on the sliding wear behaviors of graphene nanoflakes via scanning tunneling microscopy. It observed that the sliding wear states of nanoflakes were shifted from commensurate conditions to the incommensurate states at first, subsequently were slipped to another commensurate contact. When the super-small graphene sheets slid on large graphene, the torque-induced reorientation of graphene sheets might cause the superlubricity phenomenon to disappear at the homogeneous interfaces [252].

The intrinsic stability of incommensurate contact state played a significant role in friction behaviors for matching tribo-pairs of homogeneous 2D-materials. Instantaneous superlubricity at the macroscopic scale has been achieved by sliding mating steel with highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG) [253]. The experiment device was presented in Fig. 12a . After an initial sliding, multilayer graphene nanoflakes (MGNFs) formed on the steel surfaces (Fig. 12b). As indicated in Fig. 12c, the ultra-low frictional coefficient (∼0.001) appeared randomly as the wear tests continued up to the sliding distances of 105–131 μm (Fig. 12d). The sliding region actually occurred between the asperities of steel ball and HOPG; the MGNFs mainly attached onto the asperities on the steel surface, that prevented direct contacts between the steel and HOPG, as shown in Fig. 12e. Therewith, MGNFs were continuously transferred onto the asperities, the shearing plane shifted from steel/HOPG to MGNFs/HOPG, acquiring much lower shear strength [254,255]. For the extremely weak interaction between MGNFs and HOPG, the friction coefficient was reduced to 0.0003. Fig. 12f presented four contact types between the MGNFs and HOPG. However, superlubricity was obtained only at the smooth friction interface, corresponding to minimum friction energy dissipation.

Compared with homogeneous 2D-materials, heterogeneity friction pairs were able to perform lower friction forces, when the natural incommensurability contact was induced by naturally mismatched lattices in the heterostructures. Hu et al. [256] reported the ultra-low friction and wear states between the hydrophobic octadecyltrichlorosilane self-assembled monolayers (OTS-SAMs) and graphene in the adhesion-dependent tests. Adhesion forces between the graphene and OTS-SAMs rapidly descended, and the friction coefficients ultimately reached a super-low level. Furthermore, MD simulations revealed the close relationship between the twist angle and the friction properties of heterostructures [257]. Attributing to the inherent mismatching in lattice constants, a commensurate contact was demonstrated to not exist in MoS2/MoSe2 heterostructures. An incommensurate contact obtained after the rotation angles from 9 to 51 exhibited the lower friction coefficient to further achieve the superlubricity between 2D-layers.

Indeed, DFT calculations have verified that the electrostatic interactions resulting from polarizing bonds between the C atoms and functional groups greatly dominate the van der Waals interactions by altering the potential energy surface, which therefore influenced tribological performance [258]. Such as Xu et al. [259] studied the electronic structures and atomic-scale friction of graphene/ZrS2 heterostructure via first-principles calculations basing on the DFT. Analysis results revealed that potential energy corrugation of the graphene/ZrS2 heterostructure was regarded to be smaller compared with ZrS2/ZrS2 and graphene/graphene across the armchair and zigzag directions; moreover, an ultra-smooth potential energy surface was observed in such heterostructure. For its entire interface, because of interaction between atoms, the doped-electrons led to p-type doped graphene and n-type doped monolayer ZrS2. Therefore, strong electron interaction between the interfaces was found, with obvious charge redistribution; meanwhile, combining with the incommensurate contact of heterostructure, the superlow friction, i.e. superlubricity, was achieved successfully.

Noticeably, for the metal-metal friction interfaces, unconfirmed directivity of metallic bonds rendered the electrons to move freely, resulting in the strong adhesion and high shear force between tribo-interfaces. Therefore, the existing super-lubricating materials were unfit for performing the superlubricity on the metal interface. The MoWS4/graphene system provided an effective way to stimulate the nanoscroll formation, that facilitated a tribo-film to generate on the metal surface [260]. This film transformed the metal-metal tribo-contact state into tribofilm-nanoscroll-tribofilm couples, led to an incommensurate sliding interface that was crucial for the robust macroscale superlubricity. Additionally, Bakoglidis et al. [261] verified the super-lubricity behavior of carbon nitride (CNx) films (friction coefficient of 0.06), operating as self-healing material. The superlubricity of CNx was caused by CN bond rupture on its surface, led to the cross-linking removal and N2 desorption at scratch. Near the broken CN bond, the structure expanded and attained graphite-like properties, allowing these layers to serve as solid lubricants at the nanoscale and thus reducing friction. At the same time, the sub-surface layer remained basically intact with abundant of sp3-rich structures, presenting high wear-resistance. Self-healing property of the CN played a significant role in improving reliability and wear resistance of the mechanical and data storage device components from nanoscale to macro-scale.

As mentioned above, numerous superlubricity works have proven that the introduction of 2D nanomaterials (like graphene, HOPG, etc.) into tribo-interfaces is a mainstream strategy to attain superlubricity from nanoscale to macroscale. Nevertheless, high cost and uneven size control of 2D materials impede their engineering-scaled applications. In this regard, Jia et al. [250] were the first group to develop a catalytic superlubricity through in-situ generation of graphene by introducing gold on a-C:H film (Au@a-C:H) during friction. Fig. 12 g illustrated the typical HRTEM images of wear debris at different friction stages under an applied load of 5 N. At the beginning of friction test, parallel stacked graphene sheets were clearly observed (run-in state). After 8 min, the number of graphene layers decreased to 8–12 layers, and tiny graphene nanoscrolls were generated because of the bonding cooperation between graphene and carbon matrix (steady state). As the friction duration increased, many curved graphene structures formed eventually (end of the friction test). It was noteworthy that, during run-in period, the local heating induced by high friction force could melt the Au film, that promoted the diffusion of Au atoms to carbon matrix. Benefited from local flash heating, shear force, and Au atoms catalytic effects, the a-C:H matrix was triumphantly transformed into graphene structure and oriented polyolefin-like structure. Therefore, the low interfacial interactions between the graphene and hydrogen-saturated amorphous carbon matrix resulted in superlubrication (friction coefficient of approximately 0.003).

Similar to 1D-materials, 2D-nanoadditives have also been proven to be suitable for the improvements in tribological behaviors of solid and liquid lubricating systems, as well as the realization of superlubricity. According to aforementioned reports, 2D-nanoadditives with excellent ductility, toughness, and unique structure provide an efficient strategy to improve the mechanical and tribological behaviors of solid matrices. Weak interlayers van der Waals force and the strong intralayer chemical bonding endow the multilayer structure with low interlayer shear strength and excellent mechanical properties. Meanwhile, combining with the use of appropriate work conditions and reinforcement contents, these facilitate the generation of high-performance tribo-layers, thus providing the crucial aisles in improving the tribological properties. The tribo-layer consist of a single-layer, double-layer or multi-layer structure, which is present on the surface of the grain-refining layer or plastically deformed layer in the substrate. For the tribo-layer with a single-layer structure, it is considered to be the tribo-film or lubricating film, that is effective in reducing frictional resistance and increasing wear resistance. Notably, the bi-layer or multi-layer structures of the tribo-layers with excellent load-bearing and lubricating capacity also exhibit an excellent synergistic effect, achieving a significant reduction in friction resistance and material wear. In addition, for liquid lubrication systems, high surface area and easy-sliding interlayer features of 2D-nanoadditives enable them to cover worn surface defects, and play a vital role in reducing frictional resistance, as well as achieving stress transfer, which are important for the enhancements in friction and wear behaviors. In the meantime, the excellent tribological potential makes 2D-nanomaterials exhibit successful superlubricity under incommensurate contact; the heterogeneous multilayer 2D-materials have been regarded as promising candidates for achieving superlubricity, due to the mismatch of intrinsic lattice constants. These industry-related tribology achievements have significantly boosted the nanotechnology developments, while they will provide an important channel for the expansion of nanomaterials into some other new fields related to engineering-scale tribology, such as anti-wear biomaterials. It is remarkable that, complexes of multiple nanoadditives were defined as 3D-nanoadditives, which mainly comprised of 0D, 1D and 2D lubricants resulted in such materials inheriting the distinct advantages of multidimensional nanolubricants.

3D-nanoadditives mainly compose of one, two or more than three basal structural units in 0D, 1D and 2D-nanoadditives, which inherit the ascendant characteristics of their components and directly responsible for the lubricating behaviors [266–268]. Advanced nanohybrids have acquired extraordinary attentions for their demonstrated tribological potentials. Akin to the else nanoadditives, 3D-additives perform the decisive roles in improving the tribological properties of solid/liquid lubrication systems [48,269]. In this section, massive efforts to develop the advanced composites containing 3D-nanoadditives for the anti-wear enhancement and friction reduction are summarized, as well as their superlubricity contributions are also discussed. Recent works on excellent lubrication behaviors of 3D-nanoadditives have been listed in Table 4 .

Advanced 3D-nanohybrids have been affirmed to show attractive mechanical and chemical behaviors, whether applied to solid or liquid lubrication systems, are gaining ascensive attentions for their potentials in reducing friction and wear [270]. Similar to other nanomaterials, plenty of research works have developed the unique properties during wear for the 3D-materials.

Metal-based composites: Based on the concerned reports, multidimensional interaction functions of the 3D-nanocomposites, such as the inhibitory effect of 2D-nanoadditive on the agglomeration of 0D-material, the surface modification strategy of 0D-nanoparticles for nanomaterials in other dimensions, and the nanocomposite additives-induced high-performance tribofilms, etc., provide the versatile routes to solve the poor dispersion of single nanoadditive and the insufficient mechanical strength of metal matrix [271–274]. Crucial investigation of Nautiyal et al. [275] suggested that rGO-MoS2 (GM) hybrid acted as reinforcements in the copper composites to decrease friction (89%), if compared to pure copper. Splendid tribological performances mainly ascribed to incommensurate contacts between the rGO and MoS2 in the hybrid [198], that was also associated with the highly consistent and stable tribo-layer formed on the contacted interface between the Cu-GM composite and a steel ball. Furthermore, B4C/MoS2 nanohybrid (n-B4C/MoS2) rendered the Al2219 alloy to attain excellent tribological property [276]. Such nanohybrid promoted the formation of glazing layers and oxide films on a sliding surface, resulted in the separations of numerous contact surfaces that were crucial for the enhancements in frictional behaviors. Specific wear rates of Al2219 + n-B4C/MoS2 composites decreased gradually with the increase of applied temperatures from 500 to 1000 °C. According to the report of Zhang et al. [277], SiC-graphene nanosheet (GNS) also performed a crucial role in the tribological behavior of Al2O3 composite. The macroscopical coefficients of friction and wear rates for the Al2O3-composites were reduced by 29.4% and 90.1% respectively, in comparison to plain Al2O3. Contributions of GNS-rich tribofilms combined with the improved toughness and hardness, indicated the dominant factors in obtaining superior wear-resistance ability and self-lubricating properties.

Advanced Cu-carbon composites have been increasingly regarded as the ideal combinations to obtain the excellent tribological behaviors. However, poor interfacial compatibility is still an obstacle for the wide application of a composite. Silver-decorated carbon nanotube-graphite (Ag@CNTs-Gr) hybrids suggested a feasible strategy to address this dilemma [278]. Typical preparation process of Ag@CNTs-Gr/Cu composites was presented in Fig. 13a . Ag@CNTs-Gr was encapsulated by Cu2O particles through the molecular-level mixture (MLM), which optimized the CNTs-Gr dispersion in Cu matrix. As result, well-distributed reticulate CNTs-Gr improved the loading-transfer and the capacity to resist the frictional surface fractures. Broken CNTs were produced during friction rolling between two mating surfaces, and performed the visual decrease in friction coefficients. Simultaneously, the deformed and broken CNTs-Gr hybrids exposed to the worn surface resulted in forming the graphitized-tribofilm on the tribo-surface, that was of great significance in improving tribological behaviors of composites.

Furthermore, the action of 3D graphene foam (3DGFm) on friction and wear behavior has been reported well [280]. 3DGFm with porous structure played a crucial role in enhancing the anti-friction property during friction, however, they likely caused the three-body abrasion effect, which induced the destruction of tribo-layer on a sliding surface, further resulting in deterioration of tribological performance. It was worth noting that, even at a relatively high contact stress under repeated friction process, 3DGFm could still tear into 2D-graphene and formed the surface protective film, consequently preventing the direct contact between frictional pairs and improving wear resistant performance.

Polymer-based composites: Further research highlighted that with regard to 3D material-enhanced polymer systems, the tribofilm formation is increasingly regarded as a key to tribological optimization. The CuO/graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) hybrids have been synthesized via in-situ growth method [281], and then comprehendingly evaluated their tribological properties as polyimide (PI) composite additives. Analysis results proved that homodisperse g-C3N4 presented the excellent compatibility with polymer matrix, which were prone to transfer to their counterparts, promoted tribofilm formation. Subsequently, the chosen CuO endowed the tribofilm with enhanced strength that was vital in determining the high anti-wear behaviors. Hence, PI-composites with 2 wt% hybrids exhibited the optimum wear-resistance ability. Additionally, non-asbestos brakes filled with MWCNTs/zirconia nanoparticles (ZrO2) hybrids also exhibited attractive tribological potentials [282]. It discovered that MWCNTs/nano-ZrO2 hybrids have been well-dispersed in composites, and caused the formation of double-deck tribo-layer yielding extraordinarily low friction and ideal wear. The detailed results on coefficient of friction and wear rate have been listed in Table 4.

Although the specific 3D-structures have endowed the materials with distinguished friction and wear performances, it renders their mechanisms in understanding tribological behaviors to be more complicated [283,284]. Tribological mechanisms such as size effects, protective film, and third body functions were believed to be the main reasons to achieve reduced friction and wear. Hu et al. [279] explored the frictional performances of hollow spherical nanometer MoS2/TiO2 (HSMT) via a four-ball friction tester. The nano-TiO2 particles were prone to produce third-body effect in contact regions due to their low adhesion, consequently remarkable deterioration in wear was observed. Under the grinding action of nano-TiO2, the contact stress was greater than the critical peeling pressure, thus, spherical MoS2 (s-MoS2) particles with thin shell and large radius were easy to fall off, as presented in Fig. 13b. By contrast, TEM morphologies (Fig. 13c) demonstrated that s-MoS2 with the thick shell and small radius was hardly exfoliated under the same contact pressure. Exfoliated s-MoS2 tended to adhere on the metal surface to form a lubricating film, subsequently combined with the rolling nano-TiO2 to endow HSMT attractive tribological properties.

Despite the utilization of 3DGFm for tribological optimizations, it would be limited by the size and configuration of 3DGFm [285]. Recently, 3D graphene filler (3DGFr) has attained an interesting trend in the remarkable adjustment of tribological performances of polymer matrices. A convenient strategy was developed by Zhou et al. for successful synthesis of 3DGFr/epoxy resin (3DGF/EP) composites [285]. As-obtained 3DGFr products with hollow walled structures were of high length-to-thickness ratios, played a key role in obviously reducing graphene agglomeration, that potentially improved the interfacial compatibility of 3DGFr with respect to EP substrate. Important pin-on-disk friction and wear test indicated that, when adding 0.28 vol.% 3DGFr into EP matrix, the coefficient of friction reduced by ∼25%, and the specific wear rate decreased by approximately 80%, in contrast to pure EP. Raman spectra results proved that graphene in the transfer film at the surface counterface exhibited larger d-to-G peak intensity ratio (ID/IG≈1.6), as opposed to that of the original graphene foam (ID/IG≈0) [286,287]; it further indicated the transformation of graphene physicochemical structure and their involvement in the frictional process. Moreover, owing to strong interfacial adhesion of the 3DGF and EP matrix, the delamination and sliding between graphene sheets possibly appeared during friction, thereby providing an excellent ‘lubrication’ effect at the sliding interface and improving the tribo-performance.

Ceramic-based composites: Usually, ceramic hardness is well-improved at the expense of toughness, but carefully designed 3D-nanomaterials provide a conspicuous strategy for enhancing the hardness and toughness properties of composite systems. Yan et al. [288] focused on the friction and wear properties of Cr+(Cr,Al)2O3 eutectic nanocoatings and (Cr,Al)2O3 coatings. A novel 3D-nanostructure was observed within the eutectic coating. Incorporation of the rod-like nano-Cr into coatings demonstrated a noticeably increased toughness, that enabled the eutectic coating to achieve a higher wear resistance, almost 4.57 times that of (Cr,Al)2O3 coatings.

Further investigation on tribological behaviors of GO-Al2O3 hybrid reinforced ceramic has been thoroughly carried out [289]. Nano-Al2O3 successfully grafted to the GO surface via the chemical bonds, which contributed to enhancing of the bond strength of the GO cooperated with ceramic matrix. After the 15 min tests under speed of 20 mm/s and the 20 N load, the remarkable improvements in friction and wear behaviors of composites were observed. Among various GO-Al2O3 concentrations, 0.6 wt.% GO-Al2O3 rendered the friction coefficient and material wear rate of ceramics to obtain ∼18.2% and ∼61.1% reductions, respectively, as compared with those of pristine matrix.

These aforesaid investigations have proven that well-designed 3D-nanomaterials inherit the advantages of additives from 0D to 2D, and exhibit excellent dispersion, good synergy and attractive reactivity in the solid self-lubricating system; as result, it further improves the mechanical properties of solid matrix and promotes the formation of high performance tribofilm. Synergistic effects of 3D-additive components are responsible for the attractive bearing capacity and high durability of tribo-layers with the single-layer or multilayer structures.

The broad applications of 3D-nanoadditives in tribological fields have presented a high-efficiency mode for enhancing the friction and wear behaviors of liquid lubrication systems. Based on massive reports on 3D-nanolubricants, the synergistic lubrication mechanism among the additive components was considered to be the main reason to achieve splendid tribological performances [50,51].

Multiphase-assembled nanohybrids have aroused particular attentions for their tribological potentials [290]. A simple covalent bond method has been applied to prepare the boehmite (AlOOH)/graphene oxide (GO) nano-hybrid (GO-GPTS-AlOOH) [291]. Tribological explorations indicated that the homodisperse of GO-GPTS-AlOOH sheets in base oil (VHVI8) enabled them to enter and slide on the frictional interface, and the layered structure and attractive mechanical property of the nanohybrid performed a crucial role in improving the performance of raw oil. Thus, the frictional reduction and anti-wear abilities of lubricant oils containing GO-GPTS-AlOOH were dramatically improved, if compared to neat VHVI8. Particularly, their friction coefficients, wear-scar diameters and wear rates at the macroscale declined by 14%, 28% and 73%, respectively; these results have been proved in the Table 4 in detail.

Owing to naturally mismatched lattices in the unique heterostructures, attention to the lubrication performance has been attracted by 3D-nanocomposites such as MoS2/RGO [292,293]. Explorations on the friction and wear behaviors of an ionic grease containing inorganic fullerene-like MoS2/RGO (HIF-MoS2/RGO) have been conducted [294]. Results ensured the HIF-MoS2/RGO nanocomposites to show better anti-wear properties than those of commercial MoS2, single or alliances of RGO and HIF-MoS2. Especially, after wear testing at the 258 N heavy load, HIF-MoS2/RGO acquired the superior anti-wear performances. The wear rate of almost 3.1 × 10−5 mm3 was descended by 96% than that of neat grease, as shown in Table 4. Main factors that resulted in splendid tribological performance, were the self-lubricating ability of RGO and the nano-bearing effect of HIF-MoS2 nanocages.

Tribofilm formation on the worn-interface also is an important factor in reduction of friction and improvement of protection against the wear and deformation [295,296]. Song et al. [295] have proven that introducing of 0.1 wt%-Cu/PDA/GO nanocomposites into the fluid lubricant led to significant reductions in friction and wear, in comparison with those of only nano-Cu or GO addition, or Cu/GO addition; this mainly attributed to the generation of low shear-strength tribofilm containing Cu/PDA/GO nanocomposites. If compared with GO, the 2D layered nano-MoS2 sheets consisted of S-Mo-S covalent bonds are likely to exhibit better potential for tribological majorization, due to easy adsorption ability caused by the highly polarized S atoms [296]. Such as, Jia et al. [296] prepared monodisperse Cu/MoS2 nanocomposites via the in-situ chemical reduction. As shown in Fig. 14a , the anchored Cu nanoparticles have been homogeneously decorated on the PDA functionalized MoS2 nanosheets (Cu/PDA/MoS2). Tribological performances of the sunflower oil with Cu/PDA/MoS2 were investigated, and the acquired results on the friction, wear and lubrication were provided in Fig. 14b and c. Apparently, 0.5 wt% Cu/PDA/MoS2 presented an efficient approach for improving the lubrication behavior of sunflower oil. Compared to neat sunflower oil, average friction coefficient and wear-scar width of the lubricated-systems with the mixtures were decreased by 37.8% and 28.5%, respectively. Successive transfer film consisted of nano-Cu particles and MoS2 fragments played the dominant roles in enhancement of lubrication performances (Fig. 14d).

Note that the tribofilm formation can be divided into two stages. In the initial wear stage, high surface energy enabled the nanoadditives to be easily adsorbed to the friction surface and further form a protective film (physical film) without chemical reaction. With the prolongation of friction, the tribo-stress and tribo-heat were responsible for the rupture of physical film, and the film formation entered the next stage. Nano-lubricants reacted with the contact material to form a tribochemical reaction film [41].

According to the investigation of Meng et al. [297], nano-Cu/GO hybrids prepared by supercritical carbon dioxide (Sc-Cu/GO) demonstrated an effective strategy to improve tribological performance of lubricants. Anchored nano-Cu particles on GO nanosheets favored a thin protecting film to form on the contacted surface, resulting in reinforcement of load-bearing capability for base oil. Table 4 indicated that 0.05 wt% Sc-Cu/GO additives rendered the raw oil to present eminent anti-wear and friction-reducing abilities; afterwards, friction coefficients and wear-scar diameters were declined by 27.0% and 52.7%, respectively, as opposed to the pure oil. In addition, the black phosphorus dots with nano-Ag particles (Ag/BP) have also been considered as efficient additives for poly-alpha-olefin (PAO6) oil [298]. The expected deposition of Ag/BP nanoadditives in a rubbing interface led to the formation of a physical protective film. Meanwhile, the tribochemical reactions at the interface played a crucial role in generating tribofilm. Such film formed on the tirbo-contacted surface to achieve excellent frictional performances. Accordingly, friction coefficients and wear rates of the oils containing 0.075 wt% Ag/BP were reduced by 73.4% and 92.0%, respectively, as compared to the raw PAO6 oil.

With respect to the typical lubrication mechanism of eco-friendly cellulose [299], their tribological performances are closely related to their macromolecule chemical structures, as well as the tribochemical reactions during friction. Benefited from the strong hydration of cellulose chains, a surface-bound water film can easily form on the substrate surface, thus, rendering such cellulose and their derivatives to apply into lubricants widely.

Recent study emphasized that nanosized calcium borate (CB) particles were in-situ grown on a cellulose acetate-laurate (CAL), subsequently, CB/CAL nanocomposites were fabricated via hydrothermal approach [300]. When added into PAO-base oil, the CB/CAL exhibited splendid friction reduction and wear resistant performances, as experimentally proven by a four-ball tribotester. In particular, appropriate concentration of 0.6 wt% CB/CAL in the mixed lubricants resulted in significant reductions in the friction coefficients (48.7%) and average wear scar diameters (25.9%), compared to neat PAO oil. Important tribological mechanism of CB/CAL was further proposed. Under medium and low loads, CB/CAL was mainly deposited in the frictional interfaces, and played a crucial role of rolling bearing to prevent direct contact between tribo-pairs, to bear the loads of steel ball, and to disperse stress concentration. When the high loads were applied, the complex tribochemical reactions occurred at the contacting surfaces; next, a desired composite friction film consisted of Fe2O3, Fe3O4, FeO, Fe-OOH, B2O3, and CaO was generated on the tribo-surface, which could be regarded as the chemical transfer film to avoid friction and wear.

In summary, emergence of the 3D-nanomaterials likely solves the problem of poor dispersion stability for additives in fluid lubrication. The synergistic mechanism between additive components is directly responsible for excellent tribological properties of the lubrication systems, such as inhibitory effect of 2D-nanoadditive on the agglomeration of 0D-material, the surface modification strategy of 0D-nanoparticles for nanomaterials in other dimensions, and synergistically induced tribofilm. Meanwhile, tribofilm formation in liquid environment is usually divided into two steps, including the physical adsorption stage without chemical reaction, and the friction heat-induced chemical reaction stage. In the latter, the formation of high-performance tribochemical films is largely dominated by 3D-materials adsorbed on the contact surface.

Nanoadditives have been increasingly regarded as extremely attractive candidates for achieving ultra-low friction and ideal lubrication. In particular, superlubricity has been detected in the various nanoadditives, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene nanoscrolls, HOPG, MoS2 monolayer, graphene, [46,253,257] etc. Nevertheless, among the proposed materials, superlubricity is only feasible to achieve under specific conditions in laboratory. In this regard, studies have been strongly focused on developing ideally nanostructural materials for expanding their applications [301,302].

With regard to 3D-nanoadditives, crucial investigations have demonstrated that the choreographed structures were of great significance to achieve the ultra-low friction [302]. Gong et al. [303] studied tribological behaviors of graphitic-like/MoS2 films with hierarchical structure (GL/MoS2). Multilayer nanosheet structures endowed the films with excellent performances under friction and wear, rendering the macro-superlubricity (friction coefficient ∼0.004) to achieve easily under humid air atmosphere.

Gong et al. [304] fabricated nanostructure onion-like amorphous carbon (COs/a-C) film employing plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD), the scheme has been shown in Fig. 15a to understand the detailed preparation. Fig. 15b revealed that the COs/a-C films were of unique dual nanostructure, and nearly spherical carbon onions made of concentric graphite spherical shells were randomly distributed in the amorphous films. Such specific structure endowed the COs/a-C films with remarkable mechanical and tribological behaviors. The formed film showed a super-high elastic recovery up to 92%, and that obtained the super-low frictional coefficients (less than 0.01) and the ideal-small wear rate (about 6.41 × 10−9 mm3/Nm). Surface micrographs of the Al2O3 ball and COs/a-C film after wear have been provided in Fig. 15c and d. Fig. 15d showed that at the initial stage of friction, carbon flakes fell off on the film surface owing to the adsorption and bonding effect. To illustrate the nanostructure evolution and fundamental mechanisms of superlubricity, the friction mechanism models were proposed in Fig. 15e–g. Random phenomenon was profitably detected in the CO atoms sliding against arranged a-C atoms, and showed an incommensurate contact interface. Sufficient COs rendered them to fill into the mating surfaces for separating from each other, subsequently, the rolling friction and wear might play the dominate role, resulting in the anticipant realization of superlubricity (Fig. 15f and g).

In summary, the well-designed structures endow 3D-nanomaterials with the superior mechanical and tribological behaviors, that further improve the application significance of nanoadditives in the tribological field. Stable dispersibility and excellent synergy of 3D-nanoadditives compared to single-component nanoadditives usually enable them to exhibit excellent friction reduction and anti-wear properties in tribological applications in the form of solid/liquid lubrication and superlubricity. Meanwhile, the structural diversity and large compositional space of 3D-nanoadditives provide new possibilities for limitless research endeavors toward the optimization of tribo-systems.

Numerous investigations have been devoted to realizing low friction and wear between moving surfaces, which are relatively critical for the working efficiency, service life, and usage precision of mechanical systems. This review highlights the recent developments regarding the large-scale use of nanoadditives as advantageous reinforcements for tribological applications in solid, liquid, and superlubricated forms from nano-scale to macro-scales. Particularly, research works have shown nanoadditives’ superior frictional behaviors with underlying tribological mechanisms, driven by their outstanding physical and chemical properties. These continuous achievements and possible challenges have motivated the important explorations for providing massive substantial recommendations. In this section, we present the main conclusions on tribological mechanisms and the important results, as well as the future perspective and recommendations.

In recent years, the field of tribological research has focused on the development of high-performance solid/liquid lubrication systems and the realization of superlubricity. In this regard, full understandings on tribological mechanisms of nanoadditives are of great importance. In solid and liquid environments, the friction and wear behaviors of the composite systems are strongly dominated by physical and chemical phenomena such as the internal interaction of materials, the deformation, damage, and reaction of materials at the contact surface. Among these persistent phenomena, the roles of nanoadditives have received increasing attention [310,311]. For the solid matrix reinforced by nanomaterials, the friction and wear behaviors mainly depend on the chemical properties of matrixes, the dispersion of additives, the interface bonding strength with the matrix, and the tribofilm formation. As for metal matrix, the nanoadditives could refine the grain size and improve the microhardness, thus inhibiting the damage of abrasive particles or surface adhesion to the matrix. For ceramics, 0D-particles perform the role of defect repair, while 1D and 2D additives provide a feasible strategy for improving the fracture toughness of matrix. Meanwhile, 3D-materials inherit the advantages of additives from 0D to 2D, thus being responsible for the optimization in hardness and toughness of ceramics. The improved surface hardness and fracture toughness inhibit the generation of debris during friction and effectively prevent the damage of the "three-body effect" to the contact surface. Similarly, nanomaterials greatly contribute to improving the mechanical and tribological properties of polymers. 0D-material optimization mechanism drives from its nuclear capabilities, 1D-additive is related to the similar morphology with polymer chains, for the multilayer 2D-nanoadditive, the relatively low interlaminar shear strength allows them to exhibit easy-shear characteristics and excellent mechanical properties, actualizing the load transfer from the substrate to the additive. In addition, well-designed 3D-material has excellent dispersion, good synergistic effect and attractive reactivity in the polymer, which not only improves the mechanical properties of the solid matrix, but also promotes the formation of high-performance transfer film. A transfer film containing nanoadditives performs attractive adhesion, high load-carrying capacity and low shear strength, which provides an efficient approach to reduce the friction resistance of contact surface, and improve the friction and wear behavior of tribo-system. However, it should be emphasized that the realization of these optimization effects depends greatly on the dispersion of the multidimensional additive and their interfacial bonding strength with the matrix.

In addition to the solid substrates described above, multidimensional nanoadditives have also been demonstrated to be suitable for improving the tribological behavior of liquid lubrication systems, as well as achieving attractive superlubricity. Similar to solid environment, the key to achieving tribological optimization is the stable and uniform dispersion of additives. The rolling effect and surface repair mechanism of 0D-additive provide a feasible strategy to repair the contact surface morphology and promote the transition of friction state from sliding to rolling. 1D and 2D materials are also beneficial for repairing friction surface defects, and additives adsorbed on the friction surface perform a crucial role in inducing high-performance chemical reaction films. These physical and chemical phenomena are directly responsible for the optimization in lubrication performance. Different from solid matrix, the helpful formation of a tribofilm under fluid lubrication is divided into two stages: physical adsorption film and tribochemical film, according to the strength of chemical reaction. In physical adsorption stage, there is no "stress assist" and "thermal effect" in the film forming process [312]. Surface electrostatic potential dominates the formation of physical adsorption film. Subsequently, with the assistance of high pressure, friction heat and mechanical energy, the adsorption film begins to decompose and induces tribochemical reactions, indicating that the formation of the tribofilm enters the next stage. In this stage, nanoadditives are responsible for the excellent lubrication performance of the tribofilm, especially the well-designed 3D-additives, which greatly optimize the carrying capacity and durability of such film due to the synergistic effect of its components.

Tribological performances of nanocomposites mainly depend on the types of additives, dispersion, interfacial bonding strength and working conditions, etc. In this article, preliminary guidance on the selection of nanoadditives is provided for acquiring excellent lubricity, even superlubricity, in both solid/liquid lubrication systems. 0D-nanoadditives have been demonstrated to be promising solid additives, which are closely related to their unique spherical shape and high chemical stability. The incorporation of 0D-additives provides an effective approach to refine the grains of solid materials. The refined grains result in improvement of hardness that is crucial to enhance the friction and wear behaviors of the solid lubrication systems. Besides that, for the liquid lubrication, inherent limitations of fabrication technology make absolutely smooth friction surfaces non-existent at the microscopic scale. The application of nanoparticles remedies the surface defects of matching pairs and provides the “ball-bearing” effect, promoting the ideal transition from the commensurate contact to an incommensurate touch in the local interfaces. Consequently, improvements in friction reduction behavior and antiwear capability have been observed, as well as the achievement of superlubricated states.

Well-distributed 1D nano-additives enable the solid systems to achieve excellent performance under friction and wear. Similar structures to polymer chains endow them with unique advantages in improving the mechanical properties of polymer matrices; the tribofilms induced on metallic wear surfaces are responsible for the excellent antiwear behavior; in addition, for ceramic matrices, the attractive toughening mechanism also provides an efficient tribological optimization strategy. But, for the liquid lubrication systems, self-induced reaggregation of nanoadditives in the lubricants signally hinders the large-scale use of nanomaterials in trbology. Continual advanced nanotechnology offer the new opportunities to improve the solubility and dispersion of the nanoadditives without sacrificing their lubrication properties. Additionally, 1D-nanoadditives can be regarded as ideal materials for achieving the superlubricity. When the nanomaterials are applied in the incommensurate contacts, that become the “smoothest bearings”, and nearly vanished friction can be detected.

Akin to 1D-nanomaterials, the prominent mechanical strength, thermal stability, and atomically smooth surfaces render the 2D-nanoadditives to be well suitable for the wide ranges of tribological applications in forms of solid/liquid lubrication, and superlubricity. As for 2D-reinforced lubrication systems, the layered 2D-nanostructures have weak interlayer van der Waals interactions and strong intralayer chemical bonding, result in adjacent layers to be easily sheared for achieving active load transfer under extreme pressures, making them promising candidates in tribology. Moreover, 2D-nanoadditives exhibit the much higher specific surface areas compared to 1D nano-additives, which greatly determine the generation of high-performance tribofilms and the improvement of tribological behaviors.

With regard to 3D-nanoadditives, they inherit the characteristics of their components to be directly responsible for excellent lubrication. Well-designed 3D-nanoadditives provide an effective strategy to tune the limited dispersibility of components and the insufficient interfacial bonding strength in solid lubrication systems, which are closely related to their multidimensional interactions. The crucial factors leading to the superior wear-resistance and self-reducing friction of composites are the synergistic effect of nanomaterial-rich tribofilm, improved the micro/nanostructure, and enhanced mechanical behaviors.

According to the important requirements of application environments, the appropriate selection and surface treatment for nanomaterials, as well as their enhancement effects play the critical roles in optimizations of the mechanical and tribological performances of various material systems. Meanwhile, advanced applications of nano-additives represent some opportunities and challenges across the ranges of tribological systems.
(1)
Super-small size nanomaterials have aggrandized the research progress on various industrial-scale tribological applications; meanwhile, this has further motivated the multifaceted opportunities for a variety of applications, like sophisticated mechanical equipment, phase change materials, and medical treatment, etc. 0D-fullerene exhibits high reactivity potential, that renders it to apply in biomedical and cosmetic products, but the great requirements actuate the noteworthy optimization for the friction and adhesion existing in the matching interfaces between the products and skin surface. As result, designing novel interfaces at the nanoscale is required to achieve an incommensurate sliding contact to minimize the stick-slip.
(2)
At present, nano-additives can improve the anti-wear and anti-friction performances of material systems, or realize the instantaneous superlubricity. However, further research is needed to ideally achieve stable ultra-lubricity under micro/macro-scales without specific surface conditions, thus reducing negative effects from friction/wear. Such as, 1D-CNT superlubricity is found only at the nanoscale, in consequence, new experimental strategies must be further sought to bridge the discoverable gaps of the nano-scaled dimensions with respect to the macroscale behaviors.
(3)
Well-designed functional nanoadditives render them optimal candidates for adjusting the nanostructure and self-lubrication characteristics of tribo-systems. The ability to use 2D-MXenes as thin-films, composite additives, or in liquid lubricants allows MXenes to function in new applications, such as triboelectric energy generators. However, the complex mechanical behaviors of MXenes hinder their tribological applications. To achieve high-efficient tribo-systems, more fundamental researches must be conducted to better understand the underlying friction and wear mechanisms; meanwhile, finding new nanomaterials is also urgent.
(4)
3D-nanoadditives’ multiple synthetic pathways provide an exciting compositional control level; and their structural diversity and large compositional space offer new possibilities for infinite research efforts to improve frictional systems. In addition, functional diversity of 3D-nanomaterials will also contribute to solving stumbling blocks in electricity and biomedicine. However, the synthetic process complexity and high cost hinder the wide application of 3D nanomaterials in the industrial field; thus, further research should be conducted to optimize preparation process, save the costs, and control the component ratios.
(5)
Tribology has been considered as an important role in fields related to manufacturing, transportation, biology, etc. Further developing biodegradable nanoadditives with excellent friction reduction and wear-resisting properties is vital for achieving low energy consumption and excellent environmental benefits; and these properties are required for lubricants. Future research is needed to explore the bio-superlubricity behaviors of degradable nanomaterials (such as lignin, chitosan, and cellulose).

Notable prospect of this article is the successful applications of nanoadditives in the field of tribology, offering extraordinary lubricating possibilities for tribo-ystems. This article also is expected to endow the prospective tribological applications with maximal ideal possibility in anti-wear and friction reduction capabilities, and promote the further development of nanotechnology. In summary, friction-reduction behavior, wear resistant abilities, and superlubricity serve as the remarkably promising properties of functional nanoadditives for energy saving applications in the future.
